[Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")'s tenure as the [44th president of the United States](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States "List of presidents of the United States") began with [his first inauguration](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_Barack_Obama "First inauguration of Barack Obama") on January 20, 2009, and ended on January 20, 2017. Obama, a [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") from [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), took office after defeating the [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") nominee [John McCain](/wiki/John_McCain "John McCain") in the [2008 presidential election](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election"). Four years later, he won re-election in the [2012 presidential election](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election"), after defeating the Republican nominee [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney"). Alongside Obama's presidency, the Democratic Party also held their majorities in the [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") during the [111th U.S. Congress](/wiki/111th_United_States_Congress "111th United States Congress") following the [2008 elections](/wiki/2008_United_States_elections "2008 United States elections"), thereby attained an overall federal [government trifecta](/wiki/Government_trifecta#United_States "Government trifecta"). Obama is the first [African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") president, the first [multiracial](/wiki/Multiracial "Multiracial") president, the first non-white president,[[a]](#cite_note-2) and the first president born in [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"). Obama was constitutionally limited to two terms (the second re-elected Democrat President to be so) and was succeeded by Republican [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), who won the [2016 presidential election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election") against Obama's preferred successor, [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton"). Historians and political scientists rank him among the upper tier in [historical rankings of American presidents](/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#Scholar_survey_summary "Historical rankings of presidents of the United States").

Obama's accomplishments during [the first 100 days of his presidency](/wiki/First_100_days_of_the_Obama_presidency "First 100 days of the Obama presidency") included signing the [Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act of 2009](/wiki/Lilly_Ledbetter_Fair_Pay_Act_of_2009 "Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act of 2009") relaxing the [statute of limitations](/wiki/Statute_of_limitations "Statute of limitations") for equal-pay lawsuits;[[2]](#cite_note-3) signing into law the [expanded](/wiki/State_Children%27s_Health_Insurance_Program#2009_reauthorisation "State Children's Health Insurance Program") [Children's Health Insurance Program](/wiki/Children%27s_Health_Insurance_Program "Children's Health Insurance Program") (S-CHIP); winning approval of a congressional budget resolution that put Congress on record as dedicated to dealing with major [health care reform](/wiki/Health_care_reform_in_the_United_States "Health care reform in the United States") legislation in 2009; implementing new ethics guidelines designed to significantly curtail the influence of lobbyists on the executive branch; breaking from the Bush administration on a number of policy fronts, except for Iraq, in which he followed through on Bush's Iraq withdrawal of US troops;[[3]](#cite_note-CNNJK2-4) supporting the [UN declaration on sexual orientation and gender identity](/wiki/UN_declaration_on_sexual_orientation_and_gender_identity "UN declaration on sexual orientation and gender identity"); and lifting the 7½-year ban on federal funding for [embryonic stem cell research](/wiki/Embryonic_stem_cell_research "Embryonic stem cell research").[[4]](#cite_note-5) Obama also [ordered the closure](/wiki/Executive_Order_13492 "Executive Order 13492") of the [Guantanamo Bay detention camp](/wiki/Guantanamo_Bay_detention_camp "Guantanamo Bay detention camp"), in [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), though it remains open. He lifted some travel and money restrictions to the island.[[3]](#cite_note-CNNJK2-4)

Obama signed many landmark bills into law during his first two years in office. The main reforms include: the [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act"), sometimes referred to as "the ACA" or "Obamacare", the [Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act"), and the [Don't Ask, Don't Tell Repeal Act of 2010](/wiki/Don%27t_Ask,_Don%27t_Tell_Repeal_Act_of_2010 "Don't Ask, Don't Tell Repeal Act of 2010"). The [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009") and [Tax Relief, Unemployment Insurance Reauthorization, and Job Creation Act](/wiki/Tax_Relief,_Unemployment_Insurance_Reauthorization,_and_Job_Creation_Act_of_2010 "Tax Relief, Unemployment Insurance Reauthorization, and Job Creation Act of 2010") served as economic stimuli amidst the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession_in_the_United_States "Great Recession in the United States"). After a [lengthy debate over the national debt limit](/wiki/2011_United_States_debt-ceiling_crisis "2011 United States debt-ceiling crisis"), he signed the [Budget Control Act of 2011](/wiki/Budget_Control_Act_of_2011 "Budget Control Act of 2011") and the [American Taxpayer Relief Act of 2012](/wiki/American_Taxpayer_Relief_Act_of_2012 "American Taxpayer Relief Act of 2012"). In foreign policy, he increased US troop levels in [Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)"), reduced nuclear weapons with the United States–Russia [New START](/wiki/New_START "New START") treaty, and [ended military involvement](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_U.S._troops_from_Iraq_(2007%E2%80%932011) "Withdrawal of U.S. troops from Iraq (2007–2011)") in the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"). He gained widespread praise for ordering [Operation Neptune Spear](/wiki/Operation_Neptune_Spear "Operation Neptune Spear"), the raid that killed [Osama bin Laden](/wiki/Osama_bin_Laden "Osama bin Laden"), who was responsible for the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"). In 2011, Obama ordered the drone-strike killing in Yemen of [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") operative [Anwar al-Awlaki](/wiki/Anwar_al-Awlaki "Anwar al-Awlaki"), who was an American citizen. He ordered [military involvement in Libya](/wiki/2011_military_intervention_in_Libya "2011 military intervention in Libya") in order to implement [UN Security Council Resolution 1973](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_1973 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 1973"), contributing to the overthrow of [Muammar Gaddafi](/wiki/Muammar_Gaddafi "Muammar Gaddafi").

After winning re-election by defeating Republican opponent Mitt Romney, Obama was [sworn in for a second term](/wiki/Second_inauguration_of_Barack_Obama "Second inauguration of Barack Obama") on January 20, 2013. During this term, he condemned the [2013 Snowden leaks](/wiki/Global_surveillance_disclosures_(2013%E2%80%93present) "Global surveillance disclosures (2013–present)") as unpatriotic, but called for more restrictions on the [National Security Agency](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency") (NSA) to address privacy issues. Obama also promoted inclusion for [LGBT Americans](/wiki/LGBT_American "LGBT American"). His administration filed briefs that urged the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") to strike down [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_the_United_States "Same-sex marriage in the United States") bans as unconstitutional (*[United States v. Windsor](/wiki/United_States_v._Windsor "United States v. Windsor")* and *[Obergefell v. Hodges](/wiki/Obergefell_v._Hodges "Obergefell v. Hodges")*); same-sex marriage was [legalized](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_the_United_States "Same-sex marriage in the United States") nationwide in 2015 after the Court ruled so in *Obergefell*. He advocated for [gun control](/wiki/Gun_politics_in_the_United_States "Gun politics in the United States") in response to the [Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting](/wiki/Sandy_Hook_Elementary_School_shooting "Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting"), indicating support for a ban on [assault weapons](/wiki/Assault_weapon "Assault weapon"), and issued wide-ranging executive actions concerning [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming") and immigration. In foreign policy, he ordered [military interventions in Iraq](/wiki/US-led_intervention_in_Iraq_(2014%E2%80%932021) "US-led intervention in Iraq (2014–2021)") and [Syria](/wiki/US_intervention_in_the_Syrian_civil_war "US intervention in the Syrian civil war") in response to gains made by [ISIL](/wiki/Islamic_State "Islamic State") after the 2011 withdrawal from Iraq, promoted discussions that led to the 2015 [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement") on global climate change, drew down [US troops in Afghanistan](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_United_States_troops_from_Afghanistan_(2011%E2%80%932016) "Withdrawal of United States troops from Afghanistan (2011–2016)") in 2016, initiated [sanctions against Russia](/wiki/International_sanctions_during_the_Ukrainian_crisis "International sanctions during the Ukrainian crisis") following its [annexation of Crimea](/wiki/Annexation_of_Crimea_by_the_Russian_Federation "Annexation of Crimea by the Russian Federation") and again after [interference in the 2016 US elections](/wiki/Russian_interference_in_the_2016_United_States_elections "Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections"), brokered the [Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action](/wiki/Joint_Comprehensive_Plan_of_Action "Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action") nuclear deal with Iran, and [normalized US relations with Cuba](/wiki/Cuban_thaw "Cuban thaw"). Obama nominated [three justices to the Supreme Court](/wiki/Barack_Obama_Supreme_Court_candidates "Barack Obama Supreme Court candidates"): [Sonia Sotomayor](/wiki/Sonia_Sotomayor "Sonia Sotomayor") and [Elena Kagan](/wiki/Elena_Kagan "Elena Kagan") were confirmed as justices, while [Merrick Garland](/wiki/Merrick_Garland "Merrick Garland") was [denied hearings or a vote](/wiki/Merrick_Garland_Supreme_Court_nomination "Merrick Garland Supreme Court nomination") from the Republican-majority [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate").

## Milestones

### 2008 election

Main articles: [Barack Obama 2008 presidential campaign](/wiki/Barack_Obama_2008_presidential_campaign "Barack Obama 2008 presidential campaign") and [2008 United States presidential election](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election")

Further information: [2008 United States elections](/wiki/2008_United_States_elections "2008 United States elections"), [2008 Democratic Party presidential primaries](/wiki/2008_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "2008 Democratic Party presidential primaries"), and [2008 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/2008_Democratic_National_Convention "2008 Democratic National Convention")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/ElectoralCollege2008.svg/500px-ElectoralCollege2008.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ElectoralCollege2008.svg)

2008 Electoral College vote results

[Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") announced [his candidacy](/wiki/Barack_Obama_2008_presidential_campaign "Barack Obama 2008 presidential campaign") for the [Democratic nomination](/wiki/2008_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "2008 Democratic Party presidential primaries") in the [2008 presidential election](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election") on February 10, 2007.[[5]](#cite_note-ChicagoTribune_Pearson_20070210-7) In June 2008, Obama secured the Democratic nomination.[[6]](#cite_note-8) [Obama selected](/wiki/2008_Democratic_Party_vice_presidential_candidate_selection "2008 Democratic Party vice presidential candidate selection") Senator [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") of Delaware as his [running mate](/wiki/Running_mate "Running mate"), and the two were officially nominated at the [2008 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/2008_Democratic_National_Convention "2008 Democratic National Convention").

On November 4, Obama was projected to have secured the presidency. Obama won the [presidential election](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election") with 365 electoral votes and 52.9% of the popular vote, while [John McCain](/wiki/John_McCain "John McCain") received 173 electoral votes and 45.7% of the popular vote. In the concurrent [congressional elections](/wiki/2008_United_States_elections "2008 United States elections"), Democrats secured a [government trifecta](/wiki/Government_trifecta "Government trifecta") after retaining their majorities in the [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") and the [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"), and [Speaker of the House](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives") [Nancy Pelosi](/wiki/Nancy_Pelosi "Nancy Pelosi") and [Senate Majority Leader](/wiki/Party_leaders_of_the_United_States_Senate "Party leaders of the United States Senate") [Harry Reid](/wiki/Harry_Reid "Harry Reid") both remained in their posts. Republicans [John Boehner](/wiki/John_Boehner "John Boehner") and [Mitch McConnell](/wiki/Mitch_McConnell "Mitch McConnell") continued to serve as House Minority Leader and Senate Minority Leader, respectively.

### Transition period and inauguration

Main articles: [Presidential transition of Barack Obama](/wiki/Presidential_transition_of_Barack_Obama "Presidential transition of Barack Obama") and [First inauguration of Barack Obama](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_Barack_Obama "First inauguration of Barack Obama")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Official_portrait_of_Barack_Obama.jpg/120px-Official_portrait_of_Barack_Obama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_portrait_of_Barack_Obama.jpg)

Official portrait, 2009

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/President_Bush%2C_Barack_Obama_walking.jpg/250px-President_Bush%2C_Barack_Obama_walking.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Bush,_Barack_Obama_walking.jpg)

Incumbent president [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") and President-elect Obama on November 10, 2008.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/US_President_Barack_Obama_taking_his_Oath_of_Office_-_2009Jan20.jpg/250px-US_President_Barack_Obama_taking_his_Oath_of_Office_-_2009Jan20.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_President_Barack_Obama_taking_his_Oath_of_Office_-_2009Jan20.jpg)

[Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States") [John Roberts](/wiki/John_Roberts "John Roberts") administers the [presidential oath of office](/wiki/Oath_of_office_of_the_president_of_the_United_States "Oath of office of the president of the United States") to Obama at [the Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol"), January 20, 2009.

The [presidential transition period](/wiki/Presidential_transition_of_Barack_Obama "Presidential transition of Barack Obama") began following Obama's victory in the [2008 U.S. presidential election](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election"), though Obama had chosen [Chris Lu](/wiki/Chris_Lu "Chris Lu") to begin planning for the transition in May 2008.[[7]](#cite_note-bermantransition-9) [John Podesta](/wiki/John_Podesta "John Podesta"), [Valerie Jarrett](/wiki/Valerie_Jarrett "Valerie Jarrett"), and [Pete Rouse](/wiki/Pete_Rouse "Pete Rouse") co-chaired the Obama-Biden Transition Project. During the transition period, Obama announced nominations for his [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Barack_Obama "Cabinet of Barack Obama") and [administration](/wiki/Executive_Office_of_the_President_of_the_United_States "Executive Office of the President of the United States"). In November 2008, Congressman [Rahm Emanuel](/wiki/Rahm_Emanuel "Rahm Emanuel") accepted Obama's offer to serve as [White House Chief of Staff](/wiki/White_House_Chief_of_Staff "White House Chief of Staff").[[8]](#cite_note-NYT_Baker_20081106-10)

Obama was [inaugurated](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_Barack_Obama "First inauguration of Barack Obama") on January 20, 2009, officially assuming the presidency at 12:00 pm, EST,[[9]](#cite_note-11) and completing the [oath of office](/wiki/Oath_of_office_of_the_president_of_the_united_states "Oath of office of the president of the united states") at 12:05 pm, EST. He delivered his [inaugural address](/wiki/United_States_presidential_inauguration#Inaugural_address "United States presidential inauguration") immediately following his oath.[[10]](#cite_note-12) Obama's transition team was highly complimentary of the Bush administration's outgoing transition team, particularly with regards to national security, and some elements of the Bush-Obama transition were later codified into law.[[7]](#cite_note-bermantransition-9)

### First 100 days

Main article: [First 100 days of the Obama presidency](/wiki/First_100_days_of_the_Obama_presidency "First 100 days of the Obama presidency")

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Audio-input-microphone.svg/60px-Audio-input-microphone.svg.png)

[Address Before a Joint Session of Congress (February 24, 2009)](/wiki/File:Address_Before_a_Joint_Session_of_Congress_(February_24,_2009)_-_Barack_Obama_(WhiteHouse.gov).webm "File:Address Before a Joint Session of Congress (February 24, 2009) - Barack Obama (WhiteHouse.gov).webm") (52:20)

The [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009") was a focal point of President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")'s February 24, [2009 Address Before a Joint Session of Congress](/wiki/2009_Barack_Obama_speech_to_a_joint_session_of_Congress "2009 Barack Obama speech to a joint session of Congress").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Barack_Obama_signs_American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009_on_February_17.jpg/250px-Barack_Obama_signs_American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009_on_February_17.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barack_Obama_signs_American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009_on_February_17.jpg)

President Obama signs the ARRA into law on February 17, 2009 in [Denver, Colorado](/wiki/Denver "Denver"). Vice President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") stands behind him.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Barack_Obama_addresses_joint_session_of_Congress_2009-02-24.jpg/250px-Barack_Obama_addresses_joint_session_of_Congress_2009-02-24.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barack_Obama_addresses_joint_session_of_Congress_2009-02-24.jpg)

President Obama addresses a joint session of Congress, with Vice President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") and [House Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives") [Nancy Pelosi](/wiki/Nancy_Pelosi "Nancy Pelosi"), February 24, 2009.

Within minutes of Obama's taking office, his chief of staff, [Rahm Emanuel](/wiki/Rahm_Emanuel "Rahm Emanuel"), issued an order suspending last-minute regulations and [executive orders](/wiki/Executive_order "Executive order") signed by his predecessor [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush").[[11]](#cite_note-13) Some of the first actions of Obama's presidency focused on reversing measures taken by the Bush administration following the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks").[[12]](#cite_note-shearlegacy-14) In his first week in office, Obama signed [Executive Order 13492](/wiki/Executive_Order_13492 "Executive Order 13492") suspending all ongoing proceedings of the [Guantanamo military commissions](/wiki/Guantanamo_military_commission "Guantanamo military commission") and ordering the Guantanamo detention facility to be shut down within the year.[[13]](#cite_note-15) Another order, [Executive Order 13491](/wiki/Executive_Order_13491 "Executive Order 13491"), banned torture and other coercive techniques, such as [waterboarding](/wiki/Waterboarding "Waterboarding").[[14]](#cite_note-16) Obama also issued an executive order placing tighter restrictions on lobbying in the White House,[[15]](#cite_note-EthicsOrder-17) and rescinded the [Mexico City Policy](/wiki/Mexico_City_Policy "Mexico City Policy"), which banned federal grants to international groups that provide abortion services or counseling.[[16]](#cite_note-18)

On January 29, Obama signed a bill for the first time in his presidency; the [Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act of 2009](/wiki/Lilly_Ledbetter_Fair_Pay_Act_of_2009 "Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act of 2009") revised the [statute of limitations](/wiki/Statute_of_limitations "Statute of limitations") for filing [pay discrimination](/wiki/Gender_pay_gap "Gender pay gap") lawsuits.[[17]](#cite_note-19) On February 3, he signed the [Children's Health Insurance Program Reauthorization Act](/wiki/Children%27s_Health_Insurance_Program_Reauthorization_Act "Children's Health Insurance Program Reauthorization Act") (CHIP), expanding CHIP's health care coverage from 7 million children to 11 million children.[[18]](#cite_note-20) On March 9, 2009, Obama lifted restrictions on federal funding of [embryonic stem cell research](/wiki/Embryonic_stem_cell "Embryonic stem cell").[[19]](#cite_note-21) Obama stated that, like Bush, he would employ [signing statements](/wiki/Signing_statement "Signing statement") if he deems a portion of a bill to be unconstitutional,[[20]](#cite_note-22) and he subsequently issued several signing statements.[[21]](#cite_note-23) Obama also signed the [Omnibus Public Land Management Act of 2009](/wiki/Omnibus_Public_Land_Management_Act_of_2009 "Omnibus Public Land Management Act of 2009"), which added 2 million acres (8,100 km2) of land to the [National Wilderness Preservation System](/wiki/National_Wilderness_Preservation_System "National Wilderness Preservation System"),[[22]](#cite_note-landmanagement-24) as well as a law raising the cigarette pack tax by 62 cents (equivalent to $0.93 in 2025).[[23]](#cite_note-tobacco-25)

On February 17, 2009, Obama signed the [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009") (ARRA) to address the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"). The ARRA had been passed, after much debate, by both the House and Senate four days earlier. While it was originally intended to be a [bipartisan](/wiki/Bipartisan "Bipartisan") bill, Congressional passage of the bill relied largely on Democratic votes, though three Republican senators voted for it.[[24]](#cite_note-grchad-26) The lack of Republican support for the bill, and the inability of Democrats to win that support, foreshadowed the gridlock and partisanship that continued throughout Obama's presidency.[[24]](#cite_note-grchad-26)[[25]](#cite_note-mgrunstim-27)[[26]](#cite_note-obamamemos-28) The $787 billion bill combined tax breaks with spending on infrastructure projects, extension of welfare benefits, and education.[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)

### Cabinet

Main article: [Cabinet of Barack Obama](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Barack_Obama "Cabinet of Barack Obama")

See also: [Vice presidency of Joe Biden](/wiki/Vice_presidency_of_Joe_Biden "Vice presidency of Joe Biden")

Following his inauguration, Obama and the Senate worked to confirm his nominees to the [United States Cabinet](/wiki/United_States_Cabinet "United States Cabinet"). Three Cabinet-level officers did not require confirmation: Vice President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden"), whom Obama had [chosen](/wiki/2008_Democratic_Party_vice_presidential_candidate_selection "2008 Democratic Party vice presidential candidate selection") as his running mate at the [2008 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/2008_Democratic_National_Convention "2008 Democratic National Convention"), Chief of Staff [Rahm Emanuel](/wiki/Rahm_Emanuel "Rahm Emanuel"), and Defense Secretary [Robert Gates](/wiki/Robert_Gates "Robert Gates"), whom Obama chose to retain from the previous administration.[[29]](#cite_note-TheCaucus-31) An early list of suggestions came from [Michael Froman](/wiki/Michael_Froman "Michael Froman"), then an executive at [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup").[[30]](#cite_note-32) Obama described his Cabinet choices as a "[team of rivals](/wiki/Team_of_rivals "Team of rivals")", and Obama chose several prominent public officials for Cabinet positions, including defeated rival Hillary Clinton as Secretary of State.[[31]](#cite_note-syoungman-33) Obama nominated several former Clinton administration officials to the Cabinet and to other positions.[[32]](#cite_note-bsmith-34) On April 28, 2009, the Senate confirmed former Kansas governor [Kathleen Sebelius](/wiki/Kathleen_Sebelius "Kathleen Sebelius") as Secretary of Health and Human Services, completing Obama's initial Cabinet.[[33]](#cite_note-kamen_sebelius-35) During Obama's presidency, four Republicans [served](/wiki/List_of_United_States_political_appointments_across_party_lines "List of United States political appointments across party lines") in Obama's Cabinet: [Ray LaHood](/wiki/Ray_LaHood "Ray LaHood") as Secretary of Transportation, [Robert McDonald](/wiki/Robert_A._McDonald "Robert A. McDonald") as Secretary of Veterans Affairs, and Gates and [Chuck Hagel](/wiki/Chuck_Hagel "Chuck Hagel") as Secretaries of Defense.

## Judicial appointments

### United States Supreme Court nominations

Main article: [Barack Obama Supreme Court candidates](/wiki/Barack_Obama_Supreme_Court_candidates "Barack Obama Supreme Court candidates")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Obama_and_Sotomayor.jpg/250px-Obama_and_Sotomayor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Obama_and_Sotomayor.jpg)

Obama and Supreme Court nominee [Sonia Sotomayor](/wiki/Sonia_Sotomayor "Sonia Sotomayor")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Obama_signing_Kagan%27s_commission.jpg/250px-Obama_signing_Kagan%27s_commission.jpg)](/wiki/File:Obama_signing_Kagan%27s_commission.jpg)

Obama signs the commission of [Elena Kagan](/wiki/Elena_Kagan "Elena Kagan")

There were three vacancies on the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") during Obama's tenure, but Obama made only two successful appointments. During the [111th Congress](/wiki/111th_United_States_Congress "111th United States Congress"), when Democrats held a majority in the Senate, Obama successfully nominated two Supreme Court Justices:

* **[Sonia Sotomayor](/wiki/Sonia_Sotomayor "Sonia Sotomayor")**, replacing [David Souter](/wiki/David_Souter "David Souter") – [2009](/wiki/Sonia_Sotomayor_Supreme_Court_nomination "Sonia Sotomayor Supreme Court nomination")
* **[Elena Kagan](/wiki/Elena_Kagan "Elena Kagan")**, replacing [John Paul Stevens](/wiki/John_Paul_Stevens "John Paul Stevens") – [2010](/wiki/Elena_Kagan_Supreme_Court_nomination "Elena Kagan Supreme Court nomination")

Justice [Antonin Scalia](/wiki/Antonin_Scalia "Antonin Scalia") died in February 2016, during the [114th Congress](/wiki/114th_United_States_Congress "114th United States Congress"), which had a Republican majority in the Senate. In March 2016, Obama [nominated](/wiki/Merrick_Garland_Supreme_Court_nomination "Merrick Garland Supreme Court nomination") Chief Judge [Merrick Garland](/wiki/Merrick_Garland "Merrick Garland") of the [D.C. Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_District_of_Columbia_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit") to fill Scalia's seat.[[34]](#cite_note-kamita-36) However, Senate Majority Leader [Mitch McConnell](/wiki/Mitch_McConnell "Mitch McConnell"), [Judiciary Committee](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Committee_on_the_Judiciary "United States Senate Committee on the Judiciary") Chair [Chuck Grassley](/wiki/Chuck_Grassley "Chuck Grassley"), and other Senate Republicans argued that Supreme Court nominations should not be made during a presidential election year, and that the winner of the [2016 presidential election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election") should instead appoint Scalia's replacement.[[34]](#cite_note-kamita-36)[[35]](#cite_note-mshearsca-37) Garland's nomination remained before the Senate for longer than any other Supreme Court nomination in history,[[36]](#cite_note-lhurley-38) and the nomination expired with the end of the 114th Congress.[[37]](#cite_note-jbravin1-39) President Donald Trump later nominated [Neil Gorsuch](/wiki/Neil_Gorsuch "Neil Gorsuch") to Scalia's former seat on the Supreme Court, and [Gorsuch was confirmed by the Senate](/wiki/Neil_Gorsuch_Supreme_Court_nomination "Neil Gorsuch Supreme Court nomination") in April 2017.

Obama's presidency saw the continuation of battles between both parties over the confirmation of [judicial](/wiki/United_States_federal_judge "United States federal judge") nominees. Democrats continually accused Republicans of stalling nominees throughout Obama's tenure.[[39]](#cite_note-toobin1-41) After several nomination battles, Senate Democrats in 2013 reformed the use of the [filibuster](/wiki/Filibuster_in_the_United_States_Senate "Filibuster in the United States Senate") so that it could no longer be used on executive or judicial nominations (excluding the Supreme Court).[[40]](#cite_note-tmccarthy-42) Republicans took over the Senate after the [2014 elections](/wiki/2014_United_States_elections "2014 United States elections"), giving them the power to block any judicial nominee,[[41]](#cite_note-everett1-43) and the 114th Congress confirmed just 20 judicial nominees, the lowest number of confirmations since the [82nd Congress](/wiki/82nd_United_States_Congress "82nd United States Congress").[[42]](#cite_note-minkimjudges-44) Obama's judicial nominees were significantly more diverse than those of previous administrations, with more appointments going to women and minorities.[[39]](#cite_note-toobin1-41)

## Domestic affairs

See also: [Social policy of the Obama administration](/wiki/Social_policy_of_the_Obama_administration "Social policy of the Obama administration")

### Health care reform

See also: [Health care reform in the United States](/wiki/Health_care_reform_in_the_United_States "Health care reform in the United States") and [Health care reforms proposed during the Obama administration](/wiki/Health_care_reforms_proposed_during_the_Obama_administration "Health care reforms proposed during the Obama administration")

Once the stimulus bill was enacted in February 2009, health care reform became Obama's top domestic priority, and the 111th Congress passed a major bill that eventually became widely known as "[Obamacare](/wiki/Obamacare "Obamacare")". [Health care reform](/wiki/Health_care_reform_debate_in_the_United_States "Health care reform debate in the United States") had long been a top priority of the Democratic Party, and Democrats were eager to implement a new plan that would lower costs and increase coverage.[[44]](#cite_note-sack-46) In contrast to [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton")'s [1993 plan](/wiki/Clinton_health_care_plan_of_1993 "Clinton health care plan of 1993") to reform health care, Obama adopted a strategy of letting Congress drive the process, with the House and Senate writing their own bills.[[45]](#cite_note-nornstein-47) In the Senate, a bipartisan group of senators on the [Finance Committee](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Committee_on_Finance "United States Senate Committee on Finance") known as the **[Gang of Six](/wiki/Gang_of_Six#Health_care,_2009 "Gang of Six")** began meeting with the hope of creating a bipartisan healthcare reform bill,[[46]](#cite_note-herzhc-48) even though the Republican senators involved with the crafting of the bill ultimately came to oppose it.[[45]](#cite_note-nornstein-47) In November 2009, the House passed the [Affordable Health Care for America Act](/wiki/Affordable_Health_Care_for_America_Act "Affordable Health Care for America Act") on a 220–215 vote, with only one Republican voting for the bill.[[47]](#cite_note-reuterstimeline-49) In December 2009, the Senate passed its own health care reform bill, the [Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act") (PPACA or ACA), on a party-line, 60–39 vote.[[48]](#cite_note-Murray2-50) Both bills expanded [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid") and provided health care subsidies; they also established an [individual mandate](/wiki/Health_insurance_mandate#History "Health insurance mandate"), [health insurance exchanges](/wiki/Health_insurance_exchange "Health insurance exchange"), and a ban on denying coverage based on [pre-existing conditions](/wiki/Pre-existing_condition "Pre-existing condition").[[49]](#cite_note-pgrier-51) However, the House bill included a tax increase on families making more than $1 million per year and a [public health insurance option](/wiki/Public_health_insurance_option "Public health insurance option"), while the Senate plan included an [excise tax](/wiki/Excise_tax "Excise tax") on [high-cost health plans](/wiki/Cadillac_insurance_plan "Cadillac insurance plan").[[49]](#cite_note-pgrier-51)

The [2010 Massachusetts Senate special election](/wiki/2010_United_States_Senate_special_election_in_Massachusetts "2010 United States Senate special election in Massachusetts") victory of [Scott Brown](/wiki/Scott_Brown_(politician) "Scott Brown (politician)") seriously imperiled the prospects of a health care reform bill, as Democrats lost their [60-seat Senate super-majority](/wiki/Filibuster_in_the_United_States_Senate "Filibuster in the United States Senate").[[50]](#cite_note-52)[[51]](#cite_note-53) The White House and House Speaker [Nancy Pelosi](/wiki/Nancy_Pelosi "Nancy Pelosi") engaged in an extensive campaign to convince both centrists and liberals in the House to pass the Senate's health care bill, the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act.[[52]](#cite_note-bcusack-54) In March 2010, after Obama announced an [executive order](/wiki/Executive_order "Executive order") reinforcing the current law against spending federal funds for elective abortion services,[[53]](#cite_note-55) the House passed the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act.[[54]](#cite_note-housepassesaca-56) The bill, which had passed the Senate in December 2009, did not receive a single Republican vote in either house.[[54]](#cite_note-housepassesaca-56) On March 23, 2010, Obama signed the PPACA into law.[[55]](#cite_note-sgstolberg-57) *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* described the PPACA as "the most expansive social legislation enacted in decades,"[[55]](#cite_note-sgstolberg-57) while the *[Washington Post](/wiki/Washington_Post "Washington Post")* noted that it was the biggest expansion of health insurance coverage since the creation of [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(United_States) "Medicare (United States)") and [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid") in 1965.[[54]](#cite_note-housepassesaca-56) Both houses of Congress also passed a [reconciliation measure](/wiki/Health_Care_and_Education_Reconciliation_Act_of_2010 "Health Care and Education Reconciliation Act of 2010") to make significant changes and corrections to the PPACA; this second bill was signed into law on March 30, 2010.[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59) The Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act became widely known as the Affordable Care Act (ACA) or "Obamacare".[[58]](#cite_note-Obamacare-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Percentage_of_Individuals_in_the_United_States_Without_Health_Insurance%2C_1963-2015.png/250px-Percentage_of_Individuals_in_the_United_States_Without_Health_Insurance%2C_1963-2015.png)](/wiki/File:Percentage_of_Individuals_in_the_United_States_Without_Health_Insurance,_1963-2015.png)

Percentage of Individuals in the United States without Health Insurance, 1963–2015 (Source: [JAMA](/wiki/JAMA_(journal) "JAMA (journal)"))[[59]](#cite_note-61)

The Affordable Care Act faced considerable challenges and opposition after its passage, and Republicans continually attempted to repeal the law.[[60]](#cite_note-raju-62) The law also survived two major challenges that went to the Supreme Court.[[61]](#cite_note-eilroberts-63) In [National Federation of Independent Business v. Sebelius](/wiki/National_Federation_of_Independent_Business_v._Sebelius "National Federation of Independent Business v. Sebelius"), a 5–4 majority upheld the constitutionality of the Affordable Care Act, even though it made state [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid") expansion voluntary. In [King v. Burwell](/wiki/King_v._Burwell "King v. Burwell"), a 6–3 majority allowed the use of [tax credits](/wiki/Tax_credit "Tax credit") in state-operated exchanges. The October 2013 launch of [HealthCare.gov](/wiki/HealthCare.gov "HealthCare.gov"), a health insurance exchange website created under the provisions of the ACA, was widely criticized,[[62]](#cite_note-mo'brien-64) even though many of the problems were fixed by the end of the year.[[63]](#cite_note-eilperin6-65) The number of [uninsured](/wiki/Health_insurance_coverage_in_the_United_States "Health insurance coverage in the United States") Americans dropped from 20.2% of the population in 2010 to 13.3% of the population in 2015,[[64]](#cite_note-ungar-66) though Republicans continued to oppose Obamacare as an unwelcome expansion of government.[[65]](#cite_note-rpear-67) Many liberals continued to push for a [single-payer healthcare](/wiki/Single-payer_healthcare "Single-payer healthcare") system or a public option,[[52]](#cite_note-bcusack-54) and Obama endorsed the latter proposal, as well as an expansion of health insurance tax credits, in 2016.[[66]](#cite_note-mrhodan2-68)

### Wall Street reform

See also: [Banking in the United States](/wiki/Banking_in_the_United_States "Banking in the United States")

Risky practices among the major financial institutions on [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") were widely seen as contributing to the [subprime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis "Subprime mortgage crisis"), the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), and the subsequent [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), so Obama made [Wall Street reform](/wiki/Wall_Street_reform "Wall Street reform") a priority in his first term.[[67]](#cite_note-sorkin-69) On July 21, 2010, Obama signed the [Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act"), the largest financial regulatory overhaul since the [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal").[[68]](#cite_note-doddfrank-70) The act increased regulation and reporting requirements on [derivatives](/wiki/Derivative_(finance) "Derivative (finance)") (particularly [credit default swaps](/wiki/Credit_default_swap "Credit default swap")), and took steps to limit [systemic risks](/wiki/Systemic_risk "Systemic risk") to the US economy with policies such as higher [capital requirements](/wiki/Capital_requirement "Capital requirement"), the creation of the [Orderly Liquidation Authority](/wiki/Orderly_Liquidation_Authority "Orderly Liquidation Authority") to help wind down large, failing financial institutions, and the creation of the [Financial Stability Oversight Council](/wiki/Financial_Stability_Oversight_Council "Financial Stability Oversight Council") to monitor systemic risks.[[69]](#cite_note-konczal-71) Dodd-Frank also established the [Consumer Financial Protection Bureau](/wiki/Consumer_Financial_Protection_Bureau "Consumer Financial Protection Bureau"), which was charged with protecting consumers against abusive financial practices.[[70]](#cite_note-jcalmes15112015-72) On signing the bill, Obama stated that the bill would "empower consumers and investors," "bring the shadowy deals that caused the crisis to the light of day," and "put a stop to taxpayer bailouts once and for all."[[71]](#cite_note-bdennis-73) Some liberals were disappointed that the law did not break up the country's largest banks or reinstate the [Glass-Steagall Act](/wiki/Glass-Steagall_Act "Glass-Steagall Act"), while many conservatives criticized the bill as a government overreach that could make the country less competitive.[[71]](#cite_note-bdennis-73) Under the bill, the Federal Reserve and other regulatory agencies were required to propose and implement several new [regulatory rules](/wiki/Rulemaking "Rulemaking"), and battles over these rules continued throughout Obama's presidency.[[72]](#cite_note-bintelligence-74) Obama called for further Wall Street reform after the passage of Dodd-Frank, saying that banks should have a smaller role in the economy and less incentive to make risky trades.[[73]](#cite_note-goldfarb1-75) Obama also signed the [Credit CARD Act of 2009](/wiki/Credit_CARD_Act_of_2009 "Credit CARD Act of 2009"), which created new rules for credit card companies.[[74]](#cite_note-cardact-76)

### Climate change and the environment

See also: [Environmental policy of the United States § The Obama Administration (2009–2017)](/wiki/Environmental_policy_of_the_United_States#The_Obama_Administration_(2009–2017) "Environmental policy of the United States"), and [Marine policy of the Obama administration](/wiki/Marine_policy_of_the_Obama_administration "Marine policy of the Obama administration")

During his presidency, Obama described [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming") as the greatest [long-term threat](/wiki/Global_catastrophic_risk "Global catastrophic risk") facing the world.[[75]](#cite_note-trendsareterrifying-77) Obama took several steps to combat global warming, but was unable to pass a major bill addressing the issue, in part because many Republicans and some Democrats [questioned](/wiki/Climate_change_denial "Climate change denial") whether global warming is occurring and whether human activity contributes to it.[[76]](#cite_note-rglobalwarming-78) Following his inauguration, Obama asked that Congress pass a bill to put a [cap](/wiki/Emissions_trading "Emissions trading") on domestic carbon emissions.[[77]](#cite_note-lizza1-79) After the House passed the [American Clean Energy and Security Act](/wiki/American_Clean_Energy_and_Security_Act "American Clean Energy and Security Act") in 2009, Obama sought to convince the Senate to pass the bill as well.[[78]](#cite_note-ACES1-80) The legislation would have required the US to cut greenhouse gas emissions by 17 percent by 2020 and by 83 percent by the middle of the 21st century.[[78]](#cite_note-ACES1-80) However, the bill was strongly opposed by Republicans and neither it nor a separate proposed bipartisan compromise[[77]](#cite_note-lizza1-79) ever came up for a vote in the Senate.[[79]](#cite_note-walsh-81) In 2013, Obama announced that he would bypass Congress by ordering the [EPA](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency") to implement new carbon emissions limits.[[80]](#cite_note-eilperin2-82) The [Clean Power Plan](/wiki/Clean_Power_Plan "Clean Power Plan"), unveiled in 2015, seeks to reduce US greenhouse gas emissions by 26 to 28 percent by 2025.[[81]](#cite_note-davenport-83) Obama also imposed regulations on soot, sulfur, and mercury that encouraged a transition away from [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") as an energy source, but the falling price of wind, solar, and [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") energy sources also contributed to coal's decline.[[82]](#cite_note-mgrunwald-84) Obama encouraged this successful transition away from coal in large part due to the fact that coal emits more carbon than other sources of power, including natural gas.[[82]](#cite_note-mgrunwald-84)

Obama's campaign to fight global warming found more success at the international level than in Congress. Obama attended the [2009 United Nations Climate Change Conference](/wiki/2009_United_Nations_Climate_Change_Conference "2009 United Nations Climate Change Conference"), which drafted the non-binding [Copenhagen Accord](/wiki/Copenhagen_Accord "Copenhagen Accord") as a successor to the [Kyoto Protocol](/wiki/Kyoto_Protocol "Kyoto Protocol"). The deal provided for the [monitoring](/wiki/Carbon_emissions_reporting "Carbon emissions reporting") of [carbon emissions](/wiki/Carbon_emissions "Carbon emissions") among [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_countries "Developing countries"), but it did not include Obama's proposal to commit to cutting greenhouse gas emissions in half by 2050.[[83]](#cite_note-eilperin1-85) In 2014, Obama reached an agreement with China in which China pledged to reach peak carbon emission levels by 2030, while the US pledged to cut its emissions by 26–28 percent compared to its 2005 levels.[[84]](#cite_note-mlandler2-86) The deal provided momentum for a potential multilateral global warming agreement among the world's largest carbon emitters.[[85]](#cite_note-cdavenport2-87) Many Republicans criticized Obama's climate goals as a potential drain on the economy.[[85]](#cite_note-cdavenport2-87)[[86]](#cite_note-arestuccia-88) At the [2015 United Nations Climate Change Conference](/wiki/2015_United_Nations_Climate_Change_Conference "2015 United Nations Climate Change Conference"), nearly every country in the world agreed to a landmark climate deal in which each nation committed lowering their greenhouse gas emissions.[[87]](#cite_note-parisdeal-89)[[88]](#cite_note-smufson-90) The [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement") created a universal accounting system for emissions, required each country to monitor its emissions, and required each country to create a plan to reduce its emissions.[[87]](#cite_note-parisdeal-89)[[89]](#cite_note-91) Several climate negotiators noted that the US-China climate deal and the EPA's emission limits helped make the deal possible.[[87]](#cite_note-parisdeal-89) In 2016, the international community agreed to the Kigali accord, an amendment to the [Montreal Protocol](/wiki/Montreal_Protocol "Montreal Protocol") which sought to reduce the use of [HFCs](/wiki/Hydrofluorocarbons "Hydrofluorocarbons"), organic compounds that contribute to global warming.[[90]](#cite_note-kigali-92)

From the beginning of his presidency, Obama took several actions to raise [vehicle fuel efficiency](/wiki/Fuel_economy_in_automobiles "Fuel economy in automobiles") in the United States. In 2009, Obama announced a plan to increase the [Corporate Average Fuel Economy](/wiki/Corporate_Average_Fuel_Economy "Corporate Average Fuel Economy") to 35 miles per US gallon (6.7 L/100 km)], a 40 percent increase from 2009 levels.[[91]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-93) Both environmentalists and auto industry officials largely welcomed the move, as the plan raised national emission standards but provided the single national efficiency standard that auto industry officials group had long desired.[[91]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-93) In 2012, Obama set even higher standards, mandating an average fuel efficiency of 54.5 miles per US gallon (4.32 L/100 km).[[92]](#cite_note-2012CAFE-94) Obama also signed the ["cash-for-clunkers"](/wiki/Car_Allowance_Rebate_System "Car Allowance Rebate System") bill, which provided incentives to consumers to trade in older, less fuel-efficient cars for more efficient cars. The [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009") provided $54 billion in funds to encourage domestic [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") production, make federal buildings more energy-efficient, improve the [electricity grid](/wiki/Electricity_grid "Electricity grid"), repair public housing, and weatherize modest-income homes.[[93]](#cite_note-95) Obama also promoted the use of [plug-in electric vehicles](/wiki/Plug-in_electric_vehicle "Plug-in electric vehicle"), and 400,000 electric cars had been sold by the end of 2015.[[94]](#cite_note-dshepardson-96)

According to a report by The American Lung Association, there was a "major improvement" in air quality under Obama.[[95]](#cite_note-97)

### Economy

Upon entering office, Obama focused on handling the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") and the subsequent [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") that had begun before his election,[[100]](#cite_note-103)[[101]](#cite_note-104) which was generally regarded as the worst economic downturn since the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[102]](#cite_note-jpuzzanghera-105) On February 17, 2009, Obama signed into law a $787 billion [economic stimulus bill](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009") that included spending for health care, infrastructure, education, various tax breaks and [incentives](/wiki/Tax_incentive "Tax incentive"), and direct assistance to individuals. The tax provisions of the law, including a $116 billion income tax cut, temporarily reduced taxes for 98% of taxpayers, bringing tax rates to their lowest levels in 60 years.[[103]](#cite_note-106)[[104]](#cite_note-107) The Obama administration would later argue that the stimulus saved the United States from a "double-dip" recession.[[105]](#cite_note-mgrunwald2-108) Obama asked for a second major stimulus package in December 2009,[[106]](#cite_note-mscherer-109) but no major second stimulus bill passed. Obama also launched a [second bailout](/wiki/Automotive_industry_crisis_of_2008%E2%80%9310 "Automotive industry crisis of 2008–10") of US automakers, possibly saving [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") and [Chrysler](/wiki/Chrysler "Chrysler") from bankruptcy at the cost of $9.3 billion.[[107]](#cite_note-110)[[108]](#cite_note-snavely-111) For homeowners in danger of defaulting on their [mortgage](/wiki/Mortgage "Mortgage") due to the [subprime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis "Subprime mortgage crisis"), Obama launched several programs, including [HARP](/wiki/Home_Affordable_Refinance_Program "Home Affordable Refinance Program") and [HAMP](/wiki/Home_Affordable_Modification_Program "Home Affordable Modification Program").[[109]](#cite_note-hscorecard-112)[[110]](#cite_note-korton-113) Obama re-appointed [Ben Bernanke](/wiki/Ben_Bernanke "Ben Bernanke") as Chair of the [Federal Reserve Board](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_Board "Federal Reserve Board") in 2009,[[111]](#cite_note-nirwin-114) and appointed [Janet Yellen](/wiki/Janet_Yellen "Janet Yellen") to succeed Bernanke in 2013.[[112]](#cite_note-yellen-115) Short-term [interest rates](/wiki/Interest_rate "Interest rate") remained [near zero](/wiki/Zero_interest-rate_policy "Zero interest-rate policy") for much of Obama's presidency, and the Federal Reserve did not raise interest rates during Obama's presidency until December 2015.[[113]](#cite_note-2015interestrates-116)

There was a [sustained increase of the US unemployment rate](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_unemployment_rate "List of U.S. states by unemployment rate") during the early months of the administration,[[114]](#cite_note-117) as multi-year economic stimulus efforts continued.[[115]](#cite_note-118)[[116]](#cite_note-119) The unemployment rate reached a peak in October 2009 at 10.0%.[[117]](#cite_note-120) However, the economy added non-farm jobs for a record 75 straight months between October 2010 and December 2016, and the unemployment rate fell to 4.7% in December 2016.[[118]](#cite_note-mudland1-121) The recovery from the Great Recession was marked by a lower labor force participation rate, some economists attributing the lower participation rate partially to an aging population and people staying in school longer, as well as long-term structural demographic changes.[[119]](#cite_note-asoergel-122) The recovery also laid bare the growing [income inequality in the United States](/wiki/Income_inequality_in_the_United_States "Income inequality in the United States"),[[120]](#cite_note-dleonhardt-123) which the Obama administration highlighted as a major problem.[[121]](#cite_note-jharwood-124) The [federal minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage_in_the_United_States "Minimum wage in the United States") [increased](/wiki/Fair_Minimum_Wage_Act_of_2007 "Fair Minimum Wage Act of 2007") during Obama's presidency to $7.25 per hour;[[122]](#cite_note-minwage1-125) in his second term, Obama advocated for another increase to $12 per hour.[[123]](#cite_note-minwage2-126)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Obama_and_Bill_Clinton.jpg/250px-Obama_and_Bill_Clinton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Obama_and_Bill_Clinton.jpg)

Obama speaking with former president [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") and Senior Advisor [Valerie Jarrett](/wiki/Valerie_Jarrett "Valerie Jarrett") about job creation in July 2010

GDP growth returned in the third quarter of 2009, expanding at a 1.6% pace, followed by a 5.0% increase in the fourth quarter.[[124]](#cite_note-BEA1-127) Growth continued in 2010, posting an increase of 3.7% in the first quarter, with lesser gains throughout the rest of the year.[[124]](#cite_note-BEA1-127) The country's real GDP grew by about 2% in 2011, 2012, 2013, and 2014, peaking at 2.9% in 2015.[[125]](#cite_note-cnnmoneycharts-128)[[126]](#cite_note-129) In the aftermath of the recession, median household income (adjusted for inflation) declined during Obama's first term, before recovering to a new record high in his final year.[[127]](#cite_note-130) The [poverty rate](/wiki/Poverty_in_the_United_States "Poverty in the United States") peaked at 15.1% in 2010 but declined to 12.7% in 2016, which was still higher than the 12.5% pre-recession figure of 2007.[[128]](#cite_note-131)[[129]](#cite_note-132)[[130]](#cite_note-133) The relatively small GDP growth rates in the United States and other [developed countries](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") following the Great Recession left economists and others wondering whether US growth rates would ever return to the levels seen in the second half of the twentieth century.[[131]](#cite_note-leepuzzle-134)[[132]](#cite_note-lowgrowthworld-135)

### Taxation

See also: [Taxation in the United States](/wiki/Taxation_in_the_United_States "Taxation in the United States")
