Obama's presidency saw an extended battle over taxes that ultimately led to the permanent extension of most of the [Bush tax cuts](/wiki/Bush_tax_cuts "Bush tax cuts"), which had been enacted between 2001 and 2003. Those tax cuts were set to expire during Obama's presidency since they were originally passed using a Congressional maneuver known as [reconciliation](/wiki/Reconciliation_(United_States_Congress) "Reconciliation (United States Congress)"), and had to fulfill the long-term deficit requirements of the "Byrd rule". During the [lame duck](/wiki/Lame_duck_(politics) "Lame duck (politics)") session of the [111th Congress](/wiki/111th_Congress "111th Congress"), Obama and Republicans wrangled over the ultimate fate of the cuts. Obama wanted to extend the tax cuts for taxpayers making less than $250,000 a year, while Congressional Republicans wanted a total extension of the tax cuts, and refused to support any bill that did not extend tax cuts for top earners.[[134]](#cite_note-140)[[135]](#cite_note-141) Obama and the Republican Congressional leadership reached a deal that included a two-year extension of all the tax cuts, a 13-month extension of [unemployment insurance](/wiki/Unemployment_insurance "Unemployment insurance"), a one-year reduction in the [FICA payroll tax](/wiki/Federal_Insurance_Contributions_Act_tax "Federal Insurance Contributions Act tax"), and other measures.[[136]](#cite_note-2013deal-142) Obama ultimately persuaded many wary Democrats to support the bill, though [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") and others continued to oppose it.[[137]](#cite_note-cnn-signs-143)[[138]](#cite_note-144) The $858 billion [Tax Relief, Unemployment Insurance Reauthorization, and Job Creation Act of 2010](/wiki/Tax_Relief,_Unemployment_Insurance_Reauthorization,_and_Job_Creation_Act_of_2010 "Tax Relief, Unemployment Insurance Reauthorization, and Job Creation Act of 2010") passed with bipartisan majorities in both houses of Congress and was signed into law by Obama on December 17, 2010.[[137]](#cite_note-cnn-signs-143)[[139]](#cite_note-wapo-deal-145)

Shortly after Obama's 2012 re-election, Congressional Republicans and Obama again [faced off](/wiki/United_States_fiscal_cliff "United States fiscal cliff") over the final fate of the Bush tax cuts. Republicans sought to make all tax cuts permanent, while Obama sought to extend the tax cuts only for those making under $250,000.[[140]](#cite_note-steinhauer1-146) Obama and Congressional Republicans came to an agreement on the [American Taxpayer Relief Act of 2012](/wiki/American_Taxpayer_Relief_Act_of_2012 "American Taxpayer Relief Act of 2012"), which made permanent the tax cuts for individuals making less than $400,000 a year (or less than $450,000 for couples).[[140]](#cite_note-steinhauer1-146) For earnings greater than that amount, the income tax increased from 35% to 39.6%, which was the top rate before the passage of the Bush tax cuts.[[141]](#cite_note-jyellin-147) The deal also permanently indexed the [alternative minimum tax](/wiki/Alternative_minimum_tax "Alternative minimum tax") for inflation, limited [deductions](/wiki/Tax_deduction "Tax deduction") for individuals making more than $250,000 ($300,000 for couples), permanently set the [estate tax](/wiki/Estate_tax_in_the_United_States "Estate tax in the United States") exemption at $5.12 million (indexed to inflation), and increased the top estate tax rate from 35% to 40%.[[141]](#cite_note-jyellin-147) Though many Republicans did not like the deal, the bill passed the Republican House in large part due to the fact that the failure to pass any bill would have resulted in the total expiration of the Bush tax cuts.[[140]](#cite_note-steinhauer1-146)[[142]](#cite_note-lizza2-148)

### Budget and debt ceiling

See also: [United States federal budget](/wiki/United_States_federal_budget "United States federal budget")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/John_Boehner_official_portrait.jpg/250px-John_Boehner_official_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Boehner_official_portrait.jpg)

Republican [John Boehner](/wiki/John_Boehner "John Boehner") of Ohio was the powerful [Speaker of the House](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives") in 2011–2015.

[US government debt](/wiki/National_debt_of_the_United_States "National debt of the United States") grew substantially during the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), as government revenues fell. Obama largely rejected the austerity policies followed by many European countries.[[143]](#cite_note-jcalmes17062013-149) US government debt grew from 52% of GDP when Obama took office in 2009 to 74% in 2014, with most of the growth in debt coming between 2009 and 2012.[[125]](#cite_note-cnnmoneycharts-128) In 2010, Obama ordered the creation of the [National Commission on Fiscal Responsibility and Reform](/wiki/National_Commission_on_Fiscal_Responsibility_and_Reform "National Commission on Fiscal Responsibility and Reform") (also known as the "Simpson-Bowles Commission") in order to find ways to reduce the country's debt.[[144]](#cite_note-oksb-150) The commission ultimately released a report that called for a mix of spending cuts and tax increases.[[144]](#cite_note-oksb-150) Notable recommendations of the report include a cut in [military spending](/wiki/United_States_military_spending "United States military spending"), a scaling back of tax deductions for [mortgages](/wiki/Home_mortgage_interest_deduction "Home mortgage interest deduction") and employer-provided health insurance, a raise of the [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)") retirement age, and reduced spending on Medicare, Medicaid, and federal employees.[[144]](#cite_note-oksb-150) The proposal never received a vote in Congress, but it served as a template for future plans to reduce the national debt.[[145]](#cite_note-bfaler-151)

After taking control of the House in the [2010 elections](/wiki/2010_United_States_elections "2010 United States elections"), Congressional Republicans [demanded spending cuts](/wiki/Cut,_Cap_and_Balance_Act "Cut, Cap and Balance Act") in return for raising the [United States debt ceiling](/wiki/United_States_debt_ceiling "United States debt ceiling"), the statutory limit on the total amount of debt that the [Treasury Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") can issue. The [2011 debt-ceiling crisis](/wiki/2011_United_States_debt-ceiling_crisis "2011 United States debt-ceiling crisis") developed as Obama and Congressional Democrats demanded a "clean" debt-ceiling increase that did not include spending cuts.[[146]](#cite_note-152) Though some Democrats argued that Obama could unilaterally raise the debt ceiling under the terms of the [Fourteenth Amendment](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution"),[[147]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-153) Obama chose to negotiate with Congressional Republicans. Obama and Speaker of the House [John Boehner](/wiki/John_Boehner "John Boehner") attempted to negotiate a "[grand bargain](/wiki/Grand_bargain_(United_States,_2011) "Grand bargain (United States, 2011)")" to cut the deficit, reform [entitlement programs](/wiki/Entitlement_program "Entitlement program"), and re-write the tax code, but the negotiations eventually collapsed due to ideological differences between the Democratic and Republican leaders.[[148]](#cite_note-wallsten-154)[[149]](#cite_note-mbai-155)[[150]](#cite_note-gthrush-156) Congress instead passed the [Budget Control Act of 2011](/wiki/Budget_Control_Act_of_2011 "Budget Control Act of 2011"), which raised the debt ceiling, provided for domestic and military spending cuts, and established the bipartisan [Joint Select Committee on Deficit Reduction](/wiki/United_States_Congress_Joint_Select_Committee_on_Deficit_Reduction "United States Congress Joint Select Committee on Deficit Reduction") to propose further spending cuts.[[151]](#cite_note-bmontopoli1-157) As the Joint Select Committee on Deficit Reduction failed to reach an agreement on further cuts, domestic and military spending cuts known as the ["sequester"](/wiki/United_States_budget_sequestration_in_2013 "United States budget sequestration in 2013") took effect starting in 2013.[[152]](#cite_note-sequester1-158)

In October 2013, the government [shut down for two weeks](/wiki/2013_United_States_federal_government_shutdown "2013 United States federal government shutdown") as Republicans and Democrats were unable to agree on a budget. House Republicans passed a [budget](/wiki/Continuing_Appropriations_Resolution,_2014_(H.J.Res_59) "Continuing Appropriations Resolution, 2014 (H.J.Res 59)") that would defund [Obamacare](/wiki/Obamacare "Obamacare"), but Senate Democrats refused to pass any budget that defunded Obamacare.[[153]](#cite_note-159) Meanwhile, the country faced another [debt ceiling crisis](/wiki/United_States_debt-ceiling_crisis_of_2013 "United States debt-ceiling crisis of 2013"). Ultimately the two sides agreed to a [continuing resolution](/wiki/Continuing_Appropriations_Act,_2014 "Continuing Appropriations Act, 2014") that re-opened the government and suspended the debt ceiling.[[154]](#cite_note-jweisman1-160) Months after passing the continuing resolution, Congress passed the [Bipartisan Budget Act of 2013](/wiki/Bipartisan_Budget_Act_of_2013 "Bipartisan Budget Act of 2013") and an [omnibus spending bill](/wiki/Consolidated_Appropriations_Act,_2014 "Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2014") to fund the government through 2014.[[155]](#cite_note-lmontgomery2-161) In 2015, after John Boehner announced that he would [resign](/wiki/October_2015_Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives_election "October 2015 Speaker of the United States House of Representatives election") as Speaker of the House, Congress passed a bill that set government spending targets and suspended the [debt limit](/wiki/Debt_limit "Debt limit") until after Obama left office.[[156]](#cite_note-Hers3-162)

### LGBT rights

See also: [LGBT rights in the United States](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBT rights in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/White_House_rainbow_for_SCOTUS_ruling_on_same-sex_marriage.jpg/250px-White_House_rainbow_for_SCOTUS_ruling_on_same-sex_marriage.jpg)](/wiki/File:White_House_rainbow_for_SCOTUS_ruling_on_same-sex_marriage.jpg)

The [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") lit with the LGBT rainbow flag celebrating the Supreme Court's decision in [Obergefell v. Hodges](/wiki/Obergefell_v._Hodges "Obergefell v. Hodges"), legalizing same-sex marriage in the United States, June 26, 2015

During his presidency, Obama, Congress, and the Supreme Court all contributed to a major expansion of [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") rights. In 2009, Obama signed the [Matthew Shepard and James Byrd Jr. Hate Crimes Prevention Act](/wiki/Matthew_Shepard_and_James_Byrd_Jr._Hate_Crimes_Prevention_Act "Matthew Shepard and James Byrd Jr. Hate Crimes Prevention Act"), which expanded [hate crime laws](/wiki/Hate_crime_laws_in_the_United_States "Hate crime laws in the United States") to cover crimes committed because of the victim's sexual orientation.[[157]](#cite_note-hatecrimes-163) In December 2010, Obama signed the [Don't Ask, Don't Tell Repeal Act of 2010](/wiki/Don%27t_Ask,_Don%27t_Tell_Repeal_Act_of_2010 "Don't Ask, Don't Tell Repeal Act of 2010"), which ended the military's [policy](/wiki/Don%27t_ask,_don%27t_tell "Don't ask, don't tell") of disallowing openly gay and lesbian people from openly serving in the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces").[[158]](#cite_note-164) Obama also supported the passage of [ENDA](/wiki/Employment_Non-Discrimination_Act "Employment Non-Discrimination Act"), which would ban discrimination against employees on the basis of gender or sexual identity for all companies with 15 or more employees,[[159]](#cite_note-jsink-165) and the similar but more comprehensive [Equality Act](/wiki/Equality_Act_of_2015 "Equality Act of 2015").[[160]](#cite_note-benen-166) Neither bill passed Congress. In May 2012, Obama became the first sitting president to support [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage"), shortly after Vice President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") had also expressed support for the institution.[[161]](#cite_note-gast-167) The following year, Obama appointed [Todd M. Hughes](/wiki/Todd_M._Hughes "Todd M. Hughes") to the [Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Federal_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit"), making Hughes the first openly gay federal judge in US history.[[162]](#cite_note-168) In 2015, the Supreme Court ruled that the Constitution guarantees same-sex couples the right to marry in the case of *[Obergefell v. Hodges](/wiki/Obergefell_v._Hodges "Obergefell v. Hodges")*. The Obama Administration filed an amicus brief in support of gay marriage and Obama personally congratulated the plaintiff.[[163]](#cite_note-escott-169) Obama also issued dozens of executive orders intended to help LGBT Americans,[[164]](#cite_note-onceskeptical-170) including a 2010 order that extended full benefits to same-sex partners of federal employees.[[165]](#cite_note-jdiamond-171) A [2014 order](/wiki/Executive_Order_13672 "Executive Order 13672") prohibited discrimination against employees of federal contractors on the basis of sexual orientation or gender identity.[[165]](#cite_note-jdiamond-171) In 2015, Secretary of Defense [Ash Carter](/wiki/Ash_Carter "Ash Carter") ended the ban on [women in combat](/wiki/Women_in_combat "Women in combat") roles,[[166]](#cite_note-womenincombat1-172) and in 2016, he ended the ban on [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") individuals openly serving in the military.[[167]](#cite_note-transmilitary-173) On the international stage, Obama advocated for gay rights, particularly in Africa.[[168]](#cite_note-onishi-174)

### Education

Further information: [No Child Left Behind Act § Obama reforms 2010](/wiki/No_Child_Left_Behind_Act#Obama_reforms_2010 "No Child Left Behind Act")

The Great Recession of 2008–09 caused a sharp decline in tax revenues in all cities and states. The response was to cut education budgets. Obama's $800 billion stimulus package included $100 billion for public schools, which every state used to protect its educational budget. However, in terms of sponsoring innovation, Obama and his Education Secretary [Arne Duncan](/wiki/Arne_Duncan "Arne Duncan") pursued K-12 education reform through the [Race to the Top](/wiki/Race_to_the_Top "Race to the Top") grant program. With over $15 billion of grants at stake, 34 states quickly revised their education laws according to the proposals of advanced educational reformers. In the competition points were awarded for allowing charter schools to multiply, for compensating teachers on a merit basis including student test scores, and for adopting higher educational standards. There were incentives for states to establish college and career-ready standards, which in practice meant adopting the [Common Core State Standards Initiative](/wiki/Common_Core_State_Standards_Initiative "Common Core State Standards Initiative") that had been developed on a bipartisan basis by the [National Governors Association](/wiki/National_Governors_Association "National Governors Association"), and the [Council of Chief State School Officers](/wiki/Council_of_Chief_State_School_Officers "Council of Chief State School Officers"). The criteria were not mandatory, they were incentives to improve opportunities to get a grant. Most states revised their laws accordingly, even though they realized it was unlikely they would when a highly competitive new grant. Race to the Top had strong bipartisan support, with centrist elements from both parties. It was opposed by the left wing of the Democratic Party, and by the right wing of the Republican Party, and criticized for centralizing too much power in Washington. Complaints also came from middle-class families, who were annoyed at the increasing emphasis on teaching to the test, rather than encouraging teachers to show creativity and stimulating students' imagination.[[169]](#cite_note-175)[[170]](#cite_note-176)[[171]](#cite_note-177)

Obama also advocated for universal [pre-kindergarten](/wiki/Pre-kindergarten "Pre-kindergarten") programs,[[172]](#cite_note-ebadger2-178) and two free years of community college for everyone.[[173]](#cite_note-jhdavis-179) Through her [Let's Move](/wiki/Let%27s_Move "Let's Move") program and advocacy of healthier school lunches, First Lady Michelle Obama focused attention on [childhood obesity](/wiki/Childhood_obesity "Childhood obesity"), which was three times higher in 2008 than it had been in 1974.[[174]](#cite_note-kliptak2-180) In December 2015, Obama signed the [Every Student Succeeds Act](/wiki/Every_Student_Succeeds_Act "Every Student Succeeds Act"), a bipartisan bill that reauthorized federally mandated testing but shrank the federal government's role in education, especially with regard to troubled schools.[[175]](#cite_note-ESSA-181) The law also ended the use of waivers by the Education Secretary.[[175]](#cite_note-ESSA-181) In post-secondary education, Obama signed the [Health Care and Education Reconciliation Act of 2010](/wiki/Health_Care_and_Education_Reconciliation_Act_of_2010 "Health Care and Education Reconciliation Act of 2010"), which ended the role of private banks in lending out federally insured [student loans](/wiki/Student_loan "Student loan"),[[176]](#cite_note-182) created a new income-based loan repayment plan known as [Pay as You Earn](/wiki/Pay_as_You_Earn_(PAYE)_-_Federal_Student_Loan_Relief_Program "Pay as You Earn (PAYE) - Federal Student Loan Relief Program"), and increased the amount of [Pell Grant](/wiki/Pell_Grant "Pell Grant") awards given each year.[[177]](#cite_note-CBSNews-183) He also instituted new regulations on [for-profit colleges](/wiki/For-profit_college "For-profit college"), including a "gainful employment" rule that restricted federal funding from colleges that failed to adequately prepare graduates for careers.[[178]](#cite_note-agrasgreen-184)

### Immigration

See also: [Immigration to the United States](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States")

From the beginning of his presidency, Obama supported comprehensive immigration reform, including a pathway to citizenship for many immigrants illegally residing in the United States.[[179]](#cite_note-thompson1-185) However, Congress did not pass a comprehensive immigration bill during Obama's tenure, and Obama turned to executive actions. In the 2010 lame-duck session, Obama supported passage of the [DREAM Act](/wiki/DREAM_Act "DREAM Act"), which passed the House but failed to overcome a Senate filibuster in a 55–41 vote in favor of the bill.[[180]](#cite_note-herszenhorn1-186) In 2013, the Senate passed [an immigration bill](/wiki/Border_Security,_Economic_Opportunity,_and_Immigration_Modernization_Act_of_2013 "Border Security, Economic Opportunity, and Immigration Modernization Act of 2013") with a path to citizenship, but the House did not vote on the bill.[[181]](#cite_note-lizzaimm-187)[[182]](#cite_note-ggibson-188) In 2012, Obama implemented the [DACA](/wiki/Deferred_Action_for_Childhood_Arrivals "Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals") policy, which protected roughly 700,000 illegal immigrants from deportation; the policy applies only to those who were brought to the United States before their 16th birthday.[[183]](#cite_note-markon-189) In 2014, Obama announced a [new executive order](/wiki/Deferred_Action_for_Parents_of_Americans "Deferred Action for Parents of Americans") that would have protected another four million illegal immigrants from deportation,[[184]](#cite_note-ehrenfreund-190) but the order was blocked by the Supreme Court in a [4–4 tie vote](/wiki/United_States_v._Texas_(2016) "United States v. Texas (2016)") that upheld a lower court's ruling.[[185]](#cite_note-mshear1-191) Despite executive actions to protect some individuals, deportations of illegal immigrants continued under Obama. A record high of 400,000 deportations occurred in 2012, though the number of deportations fell during Obama's second term.[[186]](#cite_note-nakamuraimm-192) In continuation of a trend that began with the passage of the [Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965](/wiki/Immigration_and_Nationality_Act_of_1965 "Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965"), the percentage of foreign-born people living in the United States reached 13.7% in 2015, higher than at any point since the early 20th century.[[187]](#cite_note-agomez1-193)[[188]](#cite_note-pewimmigration1-194) After having risen since 1990, the number of illegal immigrants living in the United States stabilized at around 11.5 million individuals during Obama's presidency, down from a peak of 12.2 million in 2007.[[189]](#cite_note-nytjan2017charts1-195)[[190]](#cite_note-krogstad1-196)

The nation's [immigrant population](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") hit a record 42.2 million in 2014.[[191]](#cite_note-197) In November 2015, Obama announced a plan to resettle at least 10,000 [Syrian refugees](/wiki/Refugees_of_the_Syrian_Civil_War "Refugees of the Syrian Civil War") in the United States.[[192]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-198)

### Energy

See also: [Energy policy of the Obama administration](/wiki/Energy_policy_of_the_Obama_administration "Energy policy of the Obama administration")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/President_Speaks_to_Crew_on_Space_Station.jpg/250px-President_Speaks_to_Crew_on_Space_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Speaks_to_Crew_on_Space_Station.jpg)

Obama makes a call to the crew of the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station").

Energy production boomed during the Obama administration.[[193]](#cite_note-cisidore-199) An increase in oil production was driven largely by a [fracking](/wiki/Fracking "Fracking") boom spurred by private investment on private land, and the Obama administration played only a small role in this development.[[193]](#cite_note-cisidore-199) The Obama administration promoted the growth of [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy"),[[194]](#cite_note-tgardner-200) and [solar power](/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power") generation tripled during Obama's presidency.[[195]](#cite_note-tcama1-201) Obama also issued numerous energy efficiency standards, contributing to a flattening of growth of the total US energy demand.[[196]](#cite_note-mooneyee1-202) In May 2010, Obama extended a moratorium on offshore drilling permits after the 2010 [Deepwater Horizon oil spill](/wiki/Deepwater_Horizon_oil_spill "Deepwater Horizon oil spill"), which was the worst oil spill in US history.[[197]](#cite_note-extmorat-203)[[198]](#cite_note-largest_in_US_hist-204) In December 2016, President Obama invoked the Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act to ban offshore oil and gas exploration in large parts of the [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic_Ocean "Arctic Ocean") and Atlantic Oceans.[[199]](#cite_note-dfears1-205)

During Obama's tenure, the battle over the [Keystone XL Pipeline](/wiki/Keystone_XL_Pipeline "Keystone XL Pipeline") became a major issue, with advocates arguing that it would contribute to economic growth and environmentalists arguing that its approval would contribute to global warming.[[200]](#cite_note-ovkp-206) The proposed 1,000-mile (1,600 km) pipeline would have connected Canada's [oil sands](/wiki/Oil_sand "Oil sand") with the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico").[[200]](#cite_note-ovkp-206) Because the pipeline crossed international boundaries, its construction required the approval of the US federal government, and the US State Department engaged in a lengthy review process.[[200]](#cite_note-ovkp-206) President Obama vetoed a bill to construct the [Keystone Pipeline](/wiki/Keystone_Pipeline "Keystone Pipeline") in February 2015, arguing that the decision of approval should rest with the executive branch.[[201]](#cite_note-207) It was the first major veto of his presidency, and Congress was unable to override it.[[202]](#cite_note-208) In November 2015, Obama announced that he would not approve of the construction of the pipeline.[[200]](#cite_note-ovkp-206) On vetoing the bill, he stated that the pipeline played an "overinflated role" in US political discourse and would have had relatively little impact on job creation or climate change.[[200]](#cite_note-ovkp-206)

### Drug policy and criminal justice reform

See also: [Criminal justice reform in the United States](/wiki/Criminal_justice_reform_in_the_United_States "Criminal justice reform in the United States") and [Federal drug policy of the United States](/wiki/Federal_drug_policy_of_the_United_States "Federal drug policy of the United States")

The Obama administration took a few steps to reform the criminal justice system at a time when many in both parties felt that the US had gone too far in incarcerating drug offenders,[[203]](#cite_note-bkeller-209) and Obama was the first president since the 1960s to preside over a reduction in the federal prison population.[[204]](#cite_note-apuzzoprisonpop-210) Obama's tenure also saw a continued decline of the [national violent crime rate](/wiki/Crime_in_the_United_States "Crime in the United States") from its peak in 1991, though there was an uptick in the violent crime rate in 2015.[[205]](#cite_note-gersteincrimerate1-211)[[206]](#cite_note-cbsvc-212) In October 2009, the US Department of Justice issued a directive to federal prosecutors in states with [medical marijuana](/wiki/Medical_marijuana "Medical marijuana") laws not to investigate or prosecute cases of marijuana use or production done in compliance with those laws.[[207]](#cite_note-213) In 2009, President Obama signed the [Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2010](/wiki/Consolidated_Appropriations_Act,_2010 "Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2010"), which repealed a 21-year-old ban on federal funding of [needle exchange programs](/wiki/Needle-exchange_programme "Needle-exchange programme").[[208]](#cite_note-214) In August 2010, Obama signed the [Fair Sentencing Act](/wiki/Fair_Sentencing_Act "Fair Sentencing Act"), which reduced the sentencing disparity between [crack cocaine](/wiki/Crack_cocaine "Crack cocaine") and powder [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine").[[209]](#cite_note-fsa-215) In 2012, Colorado and Washington became the first states to [legalize](/wiki/Cannabis_in_the_United_States#State "Cannabis in the United States") non-medical [marijuana](/wiki/Marijuana "Marijuana"),[[210]](#cite_note-Ng-216) and six more states legalized recreational marijuana by the time Obama left office.[[211]](#cite_note-watkinskenny1-217) Though any use of marijuana [remained illegal](/wiki/Legal_history_of_cannabis_in_the_United_States "Legal history of cannabis in the United States") under [federal law](/wiki/Controlled_Substances_Act "Controlled Substances Act"), the Obama administration generally chose not to prosecute those who used marijuana in states that chose to legalize it.[[212]](#cite_note-richey-218) In 2016, Obama announced that the federal government would phase out the use of [private prisons](/wiki/Private_prison "Private prison").[[213]](#cite_note-privateprisons1-219) Obama [commuted](/wiki/Pardon "Pardon") the sentences of over 1,000 individuals, a higher number of commutations than any other president, and most of Obama's commutations went to nonviolent drug offenders.[[214]](#cite_note-ledermanpardons-220)[[215]](#cite_note-shorwitz1-221)

During Obama's presidency, there was a [sharp rise in opioid mortality](/wiki/Opioid_epidemic "Opioid epidemic"). Many of the deaths – then and now – result from [fentanyl](/wiki/Fentanyl "Fentanyl") consumption where an overdose is more likely than with [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin") consumption. And many people died because they were not aware of this difference or thought that they would administer themselves heroin or a drug mixture but actually used pure fentanyl.[[216]](#cite_note-222) Health experts criticized the government's response as slow and weak.[[217]](#cite_note-223)[[218]](#cite_note-224)

### Gun control

See also: [Gun politics in the United States](/wiki/Gun_politics_in_the_United_States "Gun politics in the United States")

Upon taking office in 2009, Obama expressed support for reinstating the [Federal Assault Weapons Ban](/wiki/Federal_Assault_Weapons_Ban "Federal Assault Weapons Ban"); but did not make a strong push to pass it-or any new gun control legislation early on in his presidency.[[219]](#cite_note-eplanin1-225) During his first year in office, Obama signed into law two bills containing amendments reducing restrictions on gun owners, one which permitted guns to be transported in checked baggage on Amtrak trains[[220]](#cite_note-226) and another allowing the concealed carry of loaded firearms in [National Parks](/wiki/List_of_areas_in_the_United_States_National_Park_System "List of areas in the United States National Park System"), located in states where [concealed carry](/wiki/Concealed_carry_in_the_United_States "Concealed carry in the United States") was permitted.[[221]](#cite_note-NYT20100224-227)[[222]](#cite_note-WaTimes20100222-228)

Obama's statement on the [Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting](/wiki/Sandy_Hook_Elementary_School_shooting "Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting")

Following the December 2012 [Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting](/wiki/Sandy_Hook_Elementary_School_shooting "Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting"), Obama outlined a series of sweeping gun control proposals, urging Congress to reintroduce an expired ban on "military-style" [assault weapons](/wiki/Assault_weapons "Assault weapons"), impose limits on [ammunition magazines](/wiki/Magazine_(firearms) "Magazine (firearms)") to 10 rounds, require universal [background checks](/wiki/Background_check "Background check") for all domestic gun sales, ban the possession and sale of [armor-piercing bullets](/wiki/Armor-piercing_bullet "Armor-piercing bullet") and introduce harsher penalties for gun-traffickers.[[223]](#cite_note-229)
Despite Obama's advocacy and subsequent [mass shootings](/wiki/Mass_shooting "Mass shooting"), no major gun control bill passed Congress during Obama's presidency. Senators [Joe Manchin](/wiki/Joe_Manchin "Joe Manchin") (D-WV) and [Pat Toomey](/wiki/Pat_Toomey "Pat Toomey") (R-PA) attempted to pass a more limited gun control measure that would have expanded background checks, but the bill was blocked in the Senate.[[224]](#cite_note-itkowitz-230)

### Cybersecurity

See also: [Cyberwarfare in the United States](/wiki/Cyberwarfare_in_the_United_States "Cyberwarfare in the United States")

[Cybersecurity](/wiki/Cybersecurity "Cybersecurity") emerged as an important issue during Obama's presidency. In 2009, the Obama administration established [United States Cyber Command](/wiki/United_States_Cyber_Command "United States Cyber Command"), an armed forces sub-unified command charged with defending the military against cyber attacks.[[225]](#cite_note-jgould-231) [Sony Pictures](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Entertainment "Sony Pictures Entertainment") suffered a [major hack](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Entertainment_hack "Sony Pictures Entertainment hack") in 2014, which the US government alleges originated from [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") in retaliation for the release of the film *[The Interview](/wiki/The_Interview "The Interview")*.[[226]](#cite_note-cbennett-232) China also developed sophisticated cyber-warfare forces.[[227]](#cite_note-sharris-233) In 2015, Obama declared cyber-attacks on the US a national emergency.[[226]](#cite_note-cbennett-232) Later that year, Obama signed the [Cybersecurity Information Sharing Act](/wiki/Cybersecurity_Information_Sharing_Act "Cybersecurity Information Sharing Act") into law.[[228]](#cite_note-trisen-234) In 2016, the [Democratic National Committee](/wiki/Democratic_National_Committee "Democratic National Committee") and other US organizations were [hacked](/wiki/Democratic_National_Committee_cyber_attacks "Democratic National Committee cyber attacks"),[[229]](#cite_note-rothpriest1-235) and the FBI and CIA concluded that Russia sponsored the hacking in hopes of helping Donald Trump win the 2016 presidential election.[[230]](#cite_note-gmilleraentous1-236) The [email](/wiki/Email "Email") accounts of other prominent individuals, including former secretary of state [Colin Powell](/wiki/Colin_Powell "Colin Powell") and CIA director [John O. Brennan](/wiki/John_O._Brennan "John O. Brennan"), were also hacked, leading to new fears about the confidentiality of emails.[[231]](#cite_note-shearhacking1-237)

### Racial issues

See also: [Race and ethnicity in the United States](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States "Race and ethnicity in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Beer_summit_cheers.jpg/250px-Beer_summit_cheers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beer_summit_cheers.jpg)

"Beer Summit" at the White House, July 30, 2009

In his speeches as president, Obama did not make more overt references to race relations than his predecessors,[[232]](#cite_note-238)[[233]](#cite_note-239) but according to one study, he implemented stronger policy action on behalf of African-Americans than any president since the Nixon era.[[234]](#cite_note-240)

Following Obama's election, many pondered the existence of a "postracial America".[[235]](#cite_note-wrodgers-241)[[236]](#cite_note-shearalcindor1-242) However, lingering racial tensions quickly became apparent,[[235]](#cite_note-wrodgers-241)[[237]](#cite_note-ccil2-243) and many African-Americans expressed outrage over what they saw as "racial venom" directed at Obama's presidency.[[238]](#cite_note-jblake-244) In July 2009, prominent African-American [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") professor [Henry Louis Gates Jr.](/wiki/Henry_Louis_Gates_Jr. "Henry Louis Gates Jr."), was arrested at his [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts") home by a local police officer, sparking a [controversy](/wiki/Henry_Louis_Gates_arrest_controversy "Henry Louis Gates arrest controversy") after Obama stated that the police acted "stupidly" in handling the incident. To reduce tensions, Obama invited Gates and the police officer to the White House in what became known as the "Beer Summit".[[239]](#cite_note-245) Several other incidents during Obama's presidency sparked outrage in the [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American") community or the law enforcement community, and Obama sought to build trust between law enforcement officials and civil rights activists.[[240]](#cite_note-bridgethedivide-246) The [acquittal](/wiki/Trial_of_George_Zimmerman "Trial of George Zimmerman") of [George Zimmerman](/wiki/George_Zimmerman "George Zimmerman") following the [killing of Trayvon Martin](/wiki/Killing_of_Trayvon_Martin "Killing of Trayvon Martin") sparked national outrage, leading to Obama giving a speech in which he noted that "Trayvon Martin could have been me 35 years ago."[[241]](#cite_note-ccilliza-247) The shooting of [Michael Brown](/wiki/Shooting_of_Michael_Brown "Shooting of Michael Brown") in [Ferguson, Missouri](/wiki/Ferguson,_Missouri "Ferguson, Missouri") [sparked a wave of protests](/wiki/Ferguson_unrest "Ferguson unrest").[[242]](#cite_note-capeheart2-248) These and other events led to the birth of the [Black Lives Matter](/wiki/Black_Lives_Matter "Black Lives Matter") movement, which campaigns against violence and [systemic racism](/wiki/Institutional_racism "Institutional racism") toward [black people](/wiki/Black_people "Black people").[[242]](#cite_note-capeheart2-248) Some in the law enforcement community criticized Obama's condemnation of racial bias after incidents in which police action led to the death of African-American men, while some racial justice activists criticized Obama's expressions of empathy for the police.[[240]](#cite_note-bridgethedivide-246) Though Obama entered office reluctant to talk about race, by 2014 he began openly discussing the disadvantages faced by many members of minority groups.[[243]](#cite_note-pbacon-249) In a March 2016 Gallup poll, nearly one third of Americans said they worried "a great deal" about race relations, a higher figure than in any previous [Gallup poll](/wiki/Gallup_poll "Gallup poll") since 2001.[[244]](#cite_note-250)

### NASA space policy

Main article: [Space policy of the Obama administration](/wiki/Space_policy_of_the_Obama_administration "Space policy of the Obama administration")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/President_Obama_speaks_at_Kennedy_Space_Center.jpg/250px-President_Obama_speaks_at_Kennedy_Space_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Obama_speaks_at_Kennedy_Space_Center.jpg)

President Obama speaks at [Kennedy Space Center](/wiki/Kennedy_Space_Center "Kennedy Space Center"), April 15, 2010.

In July 2009, Obama appointed [Charles Bolden](/wiki/Charles_Bolden "Charles Bolden"), a former astronaut, as [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") Administrator.[[245]](#cite_note-251) That same year, Obama set up the [Augustine panel](/wiki/Augustine_panel "Augustine panel") to review the [Constellation program](/wiki/Constellation_program "Constellation program"). In February 2010, Obama announced that he was cutting the program from the [2011 United States federal budget](/wiki/2011_United_States_federal_budget "2011 United States federal budget"), describing it as "over budget, behind schedule, and lacking in innovation."[[246]](#cite_note-BBC_cancels_Moon_return-252)[[247]](#cite_note-WashPost_eliminates_funds-253) After the decision drew criticism in the United States, a new "Flexible path to [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars")" plan was unveiled at a space conference in April 2010.[[248]](#cite_note-misswi-254)[[249]](#cite_note-255) It included new technology programs, increased R&D spending, an increase in NASA's 2011 budget from $18.3 billion to $19 billion, a focus on the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station"), and plans to contract future transportation to [Low Earth orbit](/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit "Low Earth orbit") to private companies.[[248]](#cite_note-misswi-254) During Obama's presidency, NASA designed the [Space Launch System](/wiki/Space_Launch_System "Space Launch System") and developed the [Commercial Crew Development](/wiki/Commercial_Crew_Development "Commercial Crew Development") and [Commercial Orbital Transportation Services](/wiki/Commercial_Orbital_Transportation_Services "Commercial Orbital Transportation Services") to cooperate with private space flight companies.[[250]](#cite_note-achspace-256)[[251]](#cite_note-mwall-257) These private companies, including [SpaceX](/wiki/SpaceX "SpaceX"), [Virgin Galactic](/wiki/Virgin_Galactic "Virgin Galactic"), [Blue Origin](/wiki/Blue_Origin "Blue Origin"), [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing"), and [Bigelow Aerospace](/wiki/Bigelow_Aerospace "Bigelow Aerospace"), became increasingly active during Obama's presidency.[[252]](#cite_note-cdavenport1-258) The [Space Shuttle program](/wiki/Space_Shuttle_program "Space Shuttle program") ended in 2011, and NASA relied on the [Russian space program](/wiki/Russian_space_program "Russian space program") to launch its astronauts into orbit for the remainder of the Obama administration.[[250]](#cite_note-achspace-256)[[253]](#cite_note-dmosher1-259) Obama's presidency also saw the launch of the [Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter](/wiki/Lunar_Reconnaissance_Orbiter "Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter") and the [Mars Science Laboratory](/wiki/Mars_Science_Laboratory "Mars Science Laboratory"). In 2016, Obama called on the United States to [land a human](/wiki/Human_mission_to_Mars "Human mission to Mars") on [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") by the 2030s.[[252]](#cite_note-cdavenport1-258)

### High tech initiatives

Obama promoted various technologies and the technological prowess of the United States. The number of American adults using the [internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") grew from 74% in 2008 to 84% in 2013,[[254]](#cite_note-aperrin-260) and Obama pushed programs to extend [broadband internet](/wiki/Broadband_internet "Broadband internet") to lower income Americans.[[255]](#cite_note-kzez-261) Over the opposition of many Republicans, the [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") began regulating [internet providers](/wiki/Internet_provider "Internet provider") as [public utilities](/wiki/Public_utility "Public utility"), with the goal of protecting "[net neutrality](/wiki/Net_neutrality "Net neutrality")".[[256]](#cite_note-ruizneut-262) Obama launched [18F](/wiki/18F "18F") and the [United States Digital Service](/wiki/United_States_Digital_Service "United States Digital Service"), two organizations devoted to modernizing government [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology").[[257]](#cite_note-bnaylor-263)[[258]](#cite_note-apete-264) The stimulus package included money to build [high-speed rail](/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_the_United_States "High-speed rail in the United States") networks such as the proposed [Florida High Speed Corridor](/wiki/Florida_High_Speed_Corridor "Florida High Speed Corridor"), but political resistance and funding problems stymied those efforts.[[259]](#cite_note-laing-265) In January 2016, Obama announced a plan to invest $4 billion in the development of [self-driving cars](/wiki/Autonomous_car "Autonomous car"), as well as an initiative by the [National Highway Traffic Safety Administration](/wiki/National_Highway_Traffic_Safety_Administration "National Highway Traffic Safety Administration") to develop regulations for self-driving cars.[[260]](#cite_note-trisensdc-266) That same month, Obama called for a national effort led by Vice President Biden to develop a [cure](/wiki/Cancer_research "Cancer research") for [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer").[[261]](#cite_note-ferrisca-267) On October 19, 2016, Biden spoke at the [Edward M. Kennedy Institute for the United States Senate](/wiki/Edward_M._Kennedy_Institute_for_the_United_States_Senate "Edward M. Kennedy Institute for the United States Senate") at the [University of Massachusetts Boston](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Boston "University of Massachusetts Boston") to speak about the administration's cancer initiative.[[262]](#cite_note-268) A 2020 study in the *[American Economic Review](/wiki/The_American_Economic_Review "The American Economic Review")* found that the decision by the Obama administration to issue press releases that named and shamed facilities that violated OSHA safety and health regulations led other facilities to increase their compliance and to experience fewer workplace injuries. The study estimated that each press release had the same effect on compliance as 210 inspections.[[263]](#cite_note-269)[[264]](#cite_note-270)

## Foreign affairs

Main article: [Foreign policy of the Obama administration](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_the_Obama_administration "Foreign policy of the Obama administration")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Barack_Obama_foreign_trips.png/500px-Barack_Obama_foreign_trips.png)](/wiki/File:Barack_Obama_foreign_trips.png)

[Obama made 52 international trips](/wiki/List_of_international_presidential_trips_made_by_Barack_Obama "List of international presidential trips made by Barack Obama") to 58 different countries during his presidency.[[265]](#cite_note-271)

The Obama administration inherited a [war in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)"), a [war in Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"), and a global "[war on terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror")", all launched by Congress during the term of President [Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") in the aftermath of the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"). Upon taking office, Obama called for a "[new beginning](/wiki/A_New_Beginning_(speech) "A New Beginning (speech)")" in relations between the [Muslim world](/wiki/Muslim_world "Muslim world") and the United States,[[266]](#cite_note-272)[[267]](#cite_note-273) and he discontinued the use of the term "war on terror" in favor of the term "Overseas Contingency Operation".[[268]](#cite_note-274) Obama pursued a "light footprint" military strategy in the Middle East that emphasized [special forces](/wiki/Special_forces "Special forces"), drone strikes, and diplomacy over large [ground troop](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") occupations.[[269]](#cite_note-lightfoot-275) However, American forces continued to clash with Islamic militant organizations such as [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda"), [ISIL](/wiki/ISIL "ISIL"), and [al-Shabaab](/wiki/Al-Shabaab_(militant_group) "Al-Shabaab (militant group)")[[270]](#cite_note-eilperin5-276) under the terms of the [AUMF](/wiki/Authorization_for_Use_of_Military_Force_Against_Terrorists "Authorization for Use of Military Force Against Terrorists") passed by Congress in 2001.[[271]](#cite_note-swong-277) Though the Middle East remained important to American foreign policy, Obama pursued a "[pivot](/wiki/East_Asian_foreign_policy_of_the_Barack_Obama_administration "East Asian foreign policy of the Barack Obama administration")" to [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia").[[272]](#cite_note-FP_Pacific_Century-278)[[273]](#cite_note-bbcpivot-279) Obama also emphasized closer relations with [India](/wiki/India "India"), and was the first president to visit the country twice.[[274]](#cite_note-kumar-280) An advocate for [nuclear non-proliferation](/wiki/Nuclear_non-proliferation "Nuclear non-proliferation"), Obama successfully negotiated arms-reduction deals with Iran and Russia.[[275]](#cite_note-cparsons-281) In 2015, Obama described the [Obama Doctrine](/wiki/Obama_Doctrine "Obama Doctrine"), saying "we will [engage](/wiki/Engagement_(diplomacy) "Engagement (diplomacy)"), but we preserve all our capabilities."[[276]](#cite_note-tfriedman-282) Obama also described himself as an [internationalist](/wiki/Internationalism_(politics) "Internationalism (politics)") who rejected [isolationism](/wiki/Isolationism "Isolationism") and was influenced by [realism](/wiki/Realism_(international_relations) "Realism (international relations)") and [liberal interventionism](/wiki/Liberal_internationalism "Liberal internationalism").[[277]](#cite_note-goldbergdoctrine-283)

### Iraq and Afghanistan

Troop levels in Iraq and Afghanistan[[278]](#cite_note-284)

| Year | [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War") | [Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)") |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *2007\** | 137,000[[279]](#cite_note-nyttrooplevels-285) | 26,000[[279]](#cite_note-nyttrooplevels-285) |
| *2008\** | 154,000[[279]](#cite_note-nyttrooplevels-285) | 27,500[[279]](#cite_note-nyttrooplevels-285) |
| 2009 | 139,500[[279]](#cite_note-nyttrooplevels-285) | 34,400[[279]](#cite_note-nyttrooplevels-285) |

Main articles: [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"), [War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)"), and [AfPak](/wiki/AfPak "AfPak")

During the [2008 presidential election](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election"), Obama strongly criticized the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"),[[290]](#cite_note-broder-296) and Obama withdrew the vast majority of US soldiers in Iraq by late 2011. On taking office, Obama announced that US combat forces would [leave Iraq](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_United_States_troops_from_Iraq_(2007%E2%80%932011) "Withdrawal of United States troops from Iraq (2007–2011)") by August 2010, with 35,000–50,000 American soldiers remaining in Iraq as advisers and trainers,[[291]](#cite_note-deyoung-297) down from the roughly 150,000 American soldiers in Iraq in early 2009.[[292]](#cite_note-infigures-298) In 2008, President Bush had signed the [US–Iraq Status of Forces Agreement](/wiki/US%E2%80%93Iraq_Status_of_Forces_Agreement "US–Iraq Status of Forces Agreement"), in which the United States committed to withdrawing all forces by late 2011.[[293]](#cite_note-afordhamiraq1-299)[[294]](#cite_note-lcarrollwars1-300) Obama attempted to convince Iraqi prime minister [Nouri al-Maliki](/wiki/Nouri_al-Maliki "Nouri al-Maliki") to allow US soldiers to stay past 2011, but the large presence of American soldiers was unpopular with most Iraqis.[[293]](#cite_note-afordhamiraq1-299) By late-December 2011, only 150 American soldiers remained to serve at the [US embassy](/wiki/Embassy_of_the_United_States,_Baghdad "Embassy of the United States, Baghdad").[[280]](#cite_note-jlogan-286) However, in 2014, the US began a [campaign](/wiki/US-led_intervention_in_Iraq_(2014%E2%80%932021) "US-led intervention in Iraq (2014–2021)") against [ISIL](/wiki/Islamic_State_of_Iraq_and_the_Levant "Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant"), an Islamic extremist terrorist group operating in Iraq and Syria that grew dramatically after the withdrawal of US soldiers from Iraq and the start of the [Syrian civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war").[[295]](#cite_note-abc7Aug14-301)[[296]](#cite_note-Isis1-302) By June 2015, there were about 3500 American soldiers in Iraq serving as advisers to anti-ISIL forces in the [Iraqi civil war](/wiki/War_in_Iraq_(2013%E2%80%932017) "War in Iraq (2013–2017)"),[[297]](#cite_note-bender-303) and Obama left office with roughly 5,262 US soldiers in Iraq and 503 of them in Syria.[[298]](#cite_note-parsonshennigan1-304)

> It is unacceptable that almost seven years after nearly 3,000 Americans were killed on our soil, the terrorists who attacked us on 9/11 are still at large. Osama bin Laden and Ayman al-Zawahari are recording messages to their followers and plotting more terror. The Taliban controls parts of Afghanistan. Al Qaeda has an expanding base in Pakistan that is probably no farther from their old Afghan sanctuary than a train ride from Washington to Philadelphia. If another attack on our homeland comes, it will likely come from the same region where 9/11 was planned. And yet today, we have five times more troops in Iraq than Afghanistan.[[299]](#cite_note-305)

— Obama during his 2008 presidential campaign speech

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Barack_Obama_speaks_to_US_troops_at_Camp_Victory_4-7-09_1.JPG/250px-Barack_Obama_speaks_to_US_troops_at_Camp_Victory_4-7-09_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Barack_Obama_speaks_to_US_troops_at_Camp_Victory_4-7-09_1.JPG)

President Obama speaks with US troops at [Camp Victory](/wiki/Camp_Victory "Camp Victory"), Iraq, April 2009

Obama increased the number of American soldiers in Afghanistan during his first term before withdrawing most military personnel in his second term. On taking office, Obama announced that the [US military presence in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)") would be bolstered by 17,000 new troops by Summer 2009,[[300]](#cite_note-306) on top of the roughly 30,000 soldiers already in Afghanistan at the start of 2009.[[301]](#cite_note-tyson-307) Secretary of Defense [Robert Gates](/wiki/Robert_Gates "Robert Gates"), Secretary of State [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton"), and Joint Chiefs of Staff Chair [Michael Mullen](/wiki/Michael_Mullen "Michael Mullen") all argued for further troops, and Obama dispatched additional soldiers after a lengthy review process.[[302]](#cite_note-308)[[303]](#cite_note-pbaker-309) During this time, his administration had used the neologism [AfPak](/wiki/AfPak "AfPak") to denote Afghanistan and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") as a single theater of operations in the war on terror.[[304]](#cite_note-fpif-310) The number of American soldiers in Afghanistan would peak at 100,000 in 2010.[[279]](#cite_note-nyttrooplevels-285) In 2012, the US and Afghanistan signed a [strategic partnership agreement](/wiki/U.S.%E2%80%93Afghanistan_Strategic_Partnership_Agreement "U.S.–Afghanistan Strategic Partnership Agreement") in which the US agreed to hand over major combat operation to Afghan forces.[[305]](#cite_note-landler1-311) That same year, the Obama administration designated Afghanistan as a [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally").[[306]](#cite_note-312) In 2014, Obama announced that most troops would [leave Afghanistan](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_United_States_troops_from_Afghanistan_(2011%E2%80%932016) "Withdrawal of United States troops from Afghanistan (2011–2016)") by late 2016, with a small force remaining at the [US embassy](/wiki/Embassy_of_the_United_States,_Kabul "Embassy of the United States, Kabul").[[307]](#cite_note-landler2-313) In September 2014, [Ashraf Ghani](/wiki/Mohammad_Ashraf_Ghani "Mohammad Ashraf Ghani") succeeded [Hamid Karzai](/wiki/Hamid_Karzai "Hamid Karzai") as the [President of Afghanistan](/wiki/President_of_Afghanistan "President of Afghanistan") after the US helped negotiate a power-sharing agreement between Ghani and [Abdullah Abdullah](/wiki/Abdullah_Abdullah "Abdullah Abdullah").[[308]](#cite_note-rnordland-314) On January 1, 2015, the US military ended [Operation Enduring Freedom](/wiki/Operation_Enduring_Freedom "Operation Enduring Freedom") and began [Resolute Support Mission](/wiki/Resolute_Support_Mission "Resolute Support Mission"), in which the US shifted to more of a training role, although some combat operations continued.[[309]](#cite_note-tbowman-315) In October 2015, Obama announced that US soldiers would remain in Afghanistan indefinitely in order support the Afghan government in the [civil war](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)") against the [Taliban](/wiki/Taliban "Taliban"), al-Qaeda, and [ISIL](/wiki/Islamic_State_of_Iraq_and_the_Levant_%E2%80%93_Khorasan_Province "Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant – Khorasan Province").[[310]](#cite_note-rosenberg-316) Joint Chiefs of Staff Chair [Martin Dempsey](/wiki/Martin_Dempsey "Martin Dempsey") framed the decision to keep soldiers in Afghanistan as part of a long-term [counter-terrorism](/wiki/Counter-terrorism "Counter-terrorism") operation stretching across [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia").[[311]](#cite_note-landlerafghanwar1-317) Obama left office with roughly 8,400 US soldiers remaining in Afghanistan.[[289]](#cite_note-atilghman1-295)

### East Asia

See also: [East Asian foreign policy of the Obama administration](/wiki/East_Asian_foreign_policy_of_the_Obama_administration "East Asian foreign policy of the Obama administration")

Though other areas of the world remained important to American foreign policy, Obama pursued a "pivot" to [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), focusing the US's diplomacy and trade in the region.[[272]](#cite_note-FP_Pacific_Century-278)[[273]](#cite_note-bbcpivot-279) China's continued [emergence](/wiki/China_as_an_emerging_superpower "China as an emerging superpower") as a [major power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") was a major issue of Obama's presidency; while the two countries worked together on issues such as climate change, the [China-United States relationship](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_relations "China–United States relations") also experienced tensions regarding [territorial claims](/wiki/Territorial_disputes_in_the_South_China_Sea "Territorial disputes in the South China Sea") in the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea") and the [East China Sea](/wiki/East_China_Sea "East China Sea").[[312]](#cite_note-nakamurascs-318) In 2016, the United States hosted a summit with the [Association of Southeast Asian Nations](/wiki/Association_of_Southeast_Asian_Nations "Association of Southeast Asian Nations") (ASEAN) for the first time, reflecting the Obama administration's pursuit of closer relations with ASEAN and other Asian countries.[[313]](#cite_note-jmakinen-319) After helping to encourage openly contested [elections](/wiki/2015_Myanmar_general_election "2015 Myanmar general election") in [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), Obama lifted many US sanctions on Myanmar.[[314]](#cite_note-agowen-320)[[315]](#cite_note-davismyanmar-321) Obama also increased US military ties with [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"),[[316]](#cite_note-ap1-322) [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), increased aid to [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), and contributed to a warming of relations between [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").[[317]](#cite_note-nakamura9816-323) Obama designed the [Trans-Pacific Partnership](/wiki/Trans-Pacific_Partnership "Trans-Pacific Partnership") as the key economic pillar of the Asian pivot, though the agreement remains unratified.[[317]](#cite_note-nakamura9816-323) Obama made little progress with [relations](/wiki/North_Korea%E2%80%93United_States_relations "North Korea–United States relations") with [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"), a long-time adversary of the United States, and North Korea continued to develop its [WMD program](/wiki/North_Korea_and_weapons_of_mass_destruction "North Korea and weapons of mass destruction").[[318]](#cite_note-NK9916-324)

### Russia

See also: [NATO–Russia relations](/wiki/NATO%E2%80%93Russia_relations "NATO–Russia relations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Dmitry_Medvedev_1_April_2009-1.jpg/250px-Dmitry_Medvedev_1_April_2009-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dmitry_Medvedev_1_April_2009-1.jpg)

The first meeting between [Dmitry Medvedev](/wiki/Dmitry_Medvedev "Dmitry Medvedev") and [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") before the [G20 summit](/wiki/2009_G-20_London_summit "2009 G-20 London summit") in London on April 1, 2009

On taking office, Obama called for a "[reset](/wiki/Russian_reset "Russian reset")" in [relations](/wiki/Russia%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Russia–United States relations") with [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), which had declined following the 2008 [Russo-Georgian War](/wiki/Russo-Georgian_War "Russo-Georgian War").[[319]](#cite_note-Dorning-325) While President [Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") had successfully pushed for [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") [expansion](/wiki/Expansion_of_NATO "Expansion of NATO") into former [Eastern bloc](/wiki/Eastern_bloc "Eastern bloc") states, the early Obama era saw NATO put more of an emphasis on creating a long-term partnership with Russia.[[320]](#cite_note-mjohn-326) Obama and [Russian President](/wiki/President_of_Russia "President of Russia") [Dmitry Medvedev](/wiki/Dmitry_Medvedev "Dmitry Medvedev") worked together on a new treaty to reduce and monitor nuclear weapons, Russian accession to the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), and counterterrorism.[[319]](#cite_note-Dorning-325) On April 8, 2010, Obama and Medvedev signed the [New START](/wiki/New_START "New START") treaty, a major [nuclear arms control](/wiki/Nuclear_disarmament "Nuclear disarmament") agreement that reduced the [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon") stockpiles of both countries and provided for a monitoring regime.[[321]](#cite_note-327) In December 2010, the Senate ratified New START in a 71–26 vote, with 13 Republicans and all Democrats voting in favor of the treaty.[[322]](#cite_note-sheridan-328) In 2012, Russia joined the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") and Obama [normalized](/wiki/Permanent_normal_trade_relations "Permanent normal trade relations") trade relations with Russia.[[323]](#cite_note-dpalmer-329)

US–Russia relations declined after [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") returned to the presidency in 2012.[[319]](#cite_note-Dorning-325) Russia's [invasion](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_War "Russo-Ukrainian War") of [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") and [annexation](/wiki/Annexation_of_Crimea_by_the_Russian_Federation "Annexation of Crimea by the Russian Federation") of [Crimea](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea") in response to the [Euromaidan](/wiki/Euromaidan "Euromaidan") movement led to a strong [condemnation](/wiki/International_reactions_to_the_annexation_of_Crimea_by_the_Russian_Federation "International reactions to the annexation of Crimea by the Russian Federation") by Obama and other Western leaders, who imposed [sanctions](/wiki/International_sanctions_during_the_Ukrainian_crisis "International sanctions during the Ukrainian crisis") on Russian leaders.[[319]](#cite_note-Dorning-325)[[324]](#cite_note-torbati-330) The sanctions contributed to the [Russian financial crisis (2014–2016)](/wiki/Russian_financial_crisis_(2014%E2%80%932016) "Russian financial crisis (2014–2016)").[[325]](#cite_note-russiafincrisis-331) Some members of Congress from both parties also called for the US to arm Ukrainian forces, but Obama resisted becoming closely involved in the [War in Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas "War in Donbas").[[326]](#cite_note-steinhauer3-332) In 2016, following several [cybersecurity](/wiki/Computer_security "Computer security") incidents, the Obama administration formally accused [Russia](/wiki/Cyberwarfare_in_Russia "Cyberwarfare in Russia") of engaging in a [campaign to undermine the 2016 election](/wiki/2016_United_States_election_interference_by_Russia "2016 United States election interference by Russia"), and the administration imposed sanctions on some Russian-linked people and organizations.[[327]](#cite_note-sangerrussiasanctions1-333)[[328]](#cite_note-secretstruggle-334) In 2017, after Obama left office, [Robert Mueller](/wiki/Robert_Mueller "Robert Mueller") was appointed as [special counsel](/wiki/Special_prosecutor "Special prosecutor") to investigate Russian's involvement in the 2016 election, including [allegations of conspiracy or coordination](/wiki/Links_between_Trump_associates_and_Russian_officials "Links between Trump associates and Russian officials") between Trump's presidential campaign and Russia.[[329]](#cite_note-335) The [Mueller Report](/wiki/Mueller_Report "Mueller Report"), released in 2019, concludes that Russia undertook a sustained social media campaign and cyberhacking operation to bolster the Trump campaign.[[330]](#cite_note-336) The report did not reach a conclusion on allegations that the Trump campaign had colluded with Russia, but, according to Mueller, his investigation did not find evidence "sufficient to charge any member of the [Trump] campaign with taking part in a criminal conspiracy."[[331]](#cite_note-337)

### Israel

The relationship between Obama and [Israeli](/wiki/Israel "Israel") Prime Minister [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") (who held office for all but two months of Obama's presidency) was notably icy, with many commenting on their mutual distaste for each other.[[332]](#cite_note-jgoldberg-338)[[333]](#cite_note-scollinson-339) On taking office, Obama appointed [George J. Mitchell](/wiki/George_J._Mitchell "George J. Mitchell") as a special envoy to the Middle East to work towards a settlement of the [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict"), but Mitchell made little progress before stepping down in 2011.[[334]](#cite_note-mitchell-340) In March 2010, Secretary of State Clinton criticized the Israeli government for approving expansion of settlements in East Jerusalem.[[335]](#cite_note-341) Netanyahu strongly opposed Obama's efforts to negotiate with Iran and was seen as favoring [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney") in the [2012 US presidential election](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election").[[332]](#cite_note-jgoldberg-338) However, Obama continued the US policy of vetoing [UN](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") resolutions calling for a [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinian_people "Palestinian people") state, and the administration continued to advocate for a negotiated [two-state solution](/wiki/Two-state_solution "Two-state solution").[[336]](#cite_note-jbresnahan-342) Obama also increased aid to Israel, including a $225 million emergency aid package for the [Iron Dome](/wiki/Iron_Dome "Iron Dome") air defense program.[[337]](#cite_note-343)

During Obama's last months in office, his administration chose not to veto [United Nations Security Council Resolution 2334](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_2334 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 2334"), which urged the end of [Israeli settlement](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") in the [territories that Israel captured](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories") in the [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War") of 1967. The Obama administration argued that the abstention was consistent with long-standing American opposition to the expansion of settlements, while critics of the abstention argued that it abandoned a close US ally.[[338]](#cite_note-israelabstention1-344)

### Trade agreements

See also: [United States free trade agreements](/wiki/United_States_free_trade_agreements "United States free trade agreements")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/FTAs_with_the_United_States.svg/500px-FTAs_with_the_United_States.svg.png)](/wiki/File:FTAs_with_the_United_States.svg)

The Obama administration maintained existing trade agreements and concluded new ones with [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama"), [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea")

Like his predecessor, Obama pursued [free trade agreements](/wiki/Free_trade_agreement "Free trade agreement"), in part due to the lack of progress at the [Doha negotiations](/wiki/Doha_Development_Round "Doha Development Round") in lowering [trade barriers](/wiki/Trade_barrier "Trade barrier") worldwide.[[339]](#cite_note-kgranville-345) In October 2011, the United States entered into free trade agreements with [Colombia](/wiki/United_States%E2%80%93Colombia_Free_Trade_Agreement "United States–Colombia Free Trade Agreement"), [Panama](/wiki/Panama%E2%80%93United_States_Trade_Promotion_Agreement "Panama–United States Trade Promotion Agreement"), and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea-United_States_Free_Trade_Agreement "South Korea-United States Free Trade Agreement"). Congressional Republicans overwhelmingly supported the agreements, while Congressional Democrats cast a mix of votes.[[340]](#cite_note-applebaum-346) The three agreements had originally been negotiated by the Bush administration, but Obama re-opened negotiations with each country and changed some terms of each deal.[[340]](#cite_note-applebaum-346)

Obama promoted two significantly larger, multilateral free trade agreements: the [Trans-Pacific Partnership](/wiki/Trans-Pacific_Partnership "Trans-Pacific Partnership") (TPP) with eleven Pacific Rim countries, including Japan, Mexico, and Canada, and the proposed [Transatlantic Trade and Investment Partnership](/wiki/Transatlantic_Trade_and_Investment_Partnership "Transatlantic Trade and Investment Partnership") (TTIP) with the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").[[341]](#cite_note-kanter-347) [TPP negotiations](/wiki/Trans-Pacific_Partnership_negotiations "Trans-Pacific Partnership negotiations") began under President Bush, and Obama continued them as part of a long-term strategy that sought to refocus on rapidly growing economies in East Asia.[[342]](#cite_note-lafranchi-348) The chief administration goals in the TPP, included: (1) establishing free market capitalism as the main normative platform for economic integration in the region; (2) guaranteeing standards for intellectual property rights, especially regarding copyright, software, and technology; (3) underscore American leadership in shaping the rules and norms of the emerging global order; (4) and blocking China from establishing a rival network.[[343]](#cite_note-349)

After years of negotiations, the 12 countries reached a final agreement on the content of the TPP in October 2015,[[344]](#cite_note-nakamura-350) and the full text of the treaty was made public in November 2015.[[345]](#cite_note-tppmadepublic-351) The Obama administration was criticized from the left for a lack of transparency in the negotiations, as well as the presence of corporate representatives who assisted in the drafting process.[[346]](#cite_note-352)[[347]](#cite_note-353)[[348]](#cite_note-354) In July 2015, Congress passed a bill giving [trade promotion authority](/wiki/Trade_promotion_authority "Trade promotion authority") to the president until 2021; trade promotion authority requires Congress to vote up or down on trade agreements signed by the president, with no possibility of amendments or filibusters.[[349]](#cite_note-weisman-355) The TPP became a major campaign issue in the [2016 elections](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_elections "2016 United States presidential elections"), with both major party presidential nominees opposing its ratification.[[350]](#cite_note-tppclintontrump-356) After Obama left office, President Trump pulled the United States out of the TPP negotiations, and the remaining TPP signatories later concluded a separate free trade agreement known as the [Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership](/wiki/Comprehensive_and_Progressive_Agreement_for_Trans-Pacific_Partnership "Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership").[[351]](#cite_note-357)

In June 2011, it was reported that the US Embassy aided Levi's, Hanes contractors in their fight against an increase in [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti")'s minimum wage.[[352]](#cite_note-358)

### Guantanamo Bay detention camp

In 2002, the Bush administration established the [Guantanamo Bay detention camp](/wiki/Guantanamo_Bay_detention_camp "Guantanamo Bay detention camp") to hold alleged "[enemy combatants](/wiki/Enemy_combatant "Enemy combatant")" in a manner that did not treat the detainees as conventional [prisoners of war](/wiki/Prisoner_of_war "Prisoner of war").[[353]](#cite_note-kliptakgb-359) Obama repeatedly stated his desire to close the detention camp, arguing that the camp's extrajudicial nature provided a recruitment tool for terrorist organizations.[[353]](#cite_note-kliptakgb-359) On his first day in office, Obama instructed all military prosecutors to suspend proceedings so that the incoming administration could review the [military commission](/wiki/Guantanamo_military_commission "Guantanamo military commission") process.[[354]](#cite_note-360) On January 22, 2009, Obama signed an executive order restricting interrogators to methods listed and authorized by an [Army Field Manual](/wiki/Army_Field_Manual "Army Field Manual"),[[355]](#cite_note-361) ending the use of "[enhanced interrogation techniques](/wiki/Enhanced_interrogation_techniques "Enhanced interrogation techniques")".[[356]](#cite_note-362) In March 2009, the administration announced that it would no longer refer to prisoners at Guantanamo Bay as [enemy combatants](/wiki/Enemy_combatants "Enemy combatants"), but it also asserted that the president had the authority to detain terrorism suspects there without criminal charges.[[357]](#cite_note-363) The prisoner population of the detention camp fell from 242 in January 2009 to 91 in January 2016, in part due to the [Periodic Review Boards](/wiki/Periodic_Review_Board "Periodic Review Board") that Obama established in 2011.[[358]](#cite_note-schwartzguan-364) Many members of Congress strongly opposed plans to transfer Guantanamo detainees to prisons in US states, and the Obama administration was reluctant to send potentially dangerous prisoners to other countries, especially unstable countries such as [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen").[[359]](#cite_note-oneyeartogo-365) Though Obama continued to advocate for the closure of the detention camp,[[359]](#cite_note-oneyeartogo-365) 41 inmates remained in Guantanamo when Obama left office.[[360]](#cite_note-mryanguantanamo-366)[[361]](#cite_note-savageguantanamo1-367)
