English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[Remarks by the President on Osama bin Laden](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:Remarks_by_the_President_on_Osama_bin_Laden "s:en:Remarks by the President on Osama bin Laden")**

The Obama administration launched a successful operation that resulted in the [death of](/wiki/Killing_of_Osama_bin_Laden "Killing of Osama bin Laden") [Osama bin Laden](/wiki/Osama_bin_Laden "Osama bin Laden"), the leader of [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda"), a global [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni "Sunni") [Islamist](/wiki/Islamist "Islamist") militant organization responsible for the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") and several other [terrorist](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism") attacks.[[363]](#cite_note-369) Starting with information received in July 2010, the CIA located Osama bin Laden in a [large compound](/wiki/Osama_bin_Laden%27s_compound_in_Abbottabad "Osama bin Laden's compound in Abbottabad") in [Abbottabad](/wiki/Abbottabad "Abbottabad"), Pakistan, a suburban area 35 miles (56 km) from [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad").[[364]](#cite_note-NYT-clues-370) CIA head [Leon Panetta](/wiki/Leon_Panetta "Leon Panetta") reported this intelligence to Obama in March 2011. Meeting with his national security advisers over the course of the next six weeks, Obama rejected a plan to bomb the compound, and authorized a "surgical raid" to be conducted by United States [Navy SEALs](/wiki/Navy_SEALs "Navy SEALs"). The operation took place on May 1, 2011, resulting in the [death of bin Laden](/wiki/Killing_of_Osama_bin_Laden "Killing of Osama bin Laden") and the seizure of papers and computer drives and disks from the compound.[[365]](#cite_note-WP-binLaden-371) Bin Laden's body was identified through DNA testing, and buried at sea several hours later.[[366]](#cite_note-NYT-ObamaAnnounce-372) [Reaction to the announcement](/wiki/Reactions_to_the_death_of_Osama_bin_Laden "Reactions to the death of Osama bin Laden") was positive across party lines, including from his two predecessors [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") and [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"),[[367]](#cite_note-Bloom-binLaden-373) and from many countries around the world.[[368]](#cite_note-NPR-worldreaction-374)

### Drone warfare

See also: [Drone strikes in Pakistan](/wiki/Drone_strikes_in_Pakistan "Drone strikes in Pakistan") and [Yemeni civil war (2014–present)](/wiki/Yemeni_civil_war_(2014%E2%80%93present) "Yemeni civil war (2014–present)")

Obama expanded the [drone](/wiki/Unmanned_aerial_vehicle "Unmanned aerial vehicle") strike program begun by the Bush administration, and the Obama administration conducted drone strikes against targets in [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen"), [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia"), and, most prominently, [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan").[[369]](#cite_note-scoll-375) Though the drone strikes killed high-ranking terrorists, they were also criticized for resulting in [civilian casualties](/wiki/Civilian_casualties_from_US_drone_strikes "Civilian casualties from US drone strikes").[[370]](#cite_note-sshane-376) A 2013 Pew research poll showed that the strikes were broadly unpopular in Pakistan,[[371]](#cite_note-Pew_Research-377) and some former members of the Obama administration have criticized the strikes for causing a backlash against the United States.[[370]](#cite_note-sshane-376) However, based on 147 interviews conducted in 2015, professor Aqil Shah argued that the strikes were popular in [North Waziristan](/wiki/North_Waziristan "North Waziristan"), the area in which most of the strikes take place, and that little [blowback](/wiki/Blowback_(intelligence) "Blowback (intelligence)") occurred.[[372]](#cite_note-ashah-378) In 2009, the [UN](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") special investigator on [extrajudicial](/wiki/Extrajudicial_killing "Extrajudicial killing"), [summary, or arbitrary executions](/wiki/Extrajudicial_punishment "Extrajudicial punishment") called the United States' reliance on drones "increasingly common" and "deeply troubling", and called on the US to justify its use of targeted assassinations rather than attempting to capture al Qaeda or Taliban suspects.[[373]](#cite_note-cnn_un_probe-379)[[374]](#cite_note-380)

Starting in 2011, in response to Obama's attempts to avoid civilian casualties, the [Hellfire R9X](/wiki/AGM-114_Hellfire#Hellfire_R9X "AGM-114 Hellfire") "flying Ginsu" missile was developed. It is usually fired from drones. It does not have an explosive warhead that causes a large area of destruction but kills by using six rotating blades that cut the target into shreds. On July 31, 2022, [Al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") leader [Ayman al-Zawahiri](/wiki/Ayman_al-Zawahiri "Ayman al-Zawahiri") was killed by an R9X missile.[[375]](#cite_note-Weber_8/6/2022-381) In 2013, Obama appointed [John Brennan](/wiki/John_O._Brennan "John O. Brennan") as the new CIA Director and announced a new policy that required CIA operatives to determine with a "near-certainty" that no civilians would be hurt in a drone strike.[[369]](#cite_note-scoll-375) The number of drone strikes fell substantially after the announcement of the new policy.[[369]](#cite_note-scoll-375)[[370]](#cite_note-sshane-376)

As of 2015, US drone strikes had killed eight American citizens, one of whom, [Anwar al-Aulaqi](/wiki/Anwar_al-Aulaqi "Anwar al-Aulaqi"), was targeted.[[370]](#cite_note-sshane-376) The targeted killing of a United States citizen raised Constitutional issues, as it is the first known instance of a sitting US president ordering the [extrajudicial killing](/wiki/Extrajudicial_killing "Extrajudicial killing") of a US citizen.[[376]](#cite_note-382)[[377]](#cite_note-383) Obama had ordered the targeted killing of al-Aulaqi, a Muslim cleric with ties to [al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Al-Qaeda_in_the_Arabian_Peninsula "Al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula"), after al-Aulaqi allegedly shifted from encouraging attacks on the United States to directly participating in them.[[378]](#cite_note-shaneala-384)[[379]](#cite_note-385) The Obama administration continually sought to keep classified the legal opinions justifying drone strikes, but it said that it conducted special legal reviews before targeting Americans in order to purportedly satisfy the [due process](/wiki/Due_Process_Clause "Due Process Clause") requirements of the Constitution.[[370]](#cite_note-sshane-376)[[380]](#cite_note-NYTBeckerShane-386)

### Cuban thaw

Main article: [Cuban thaw](/wiki/Cuban_thaw "Cuban thaw")

See also: [Cuba–United States relations](/wiki/Cuba%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Cuba–United States relations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Handshake_between_the_President_and_Cuban_President_Ra%C3%BAl_Castro.jpg/250px-Handshake_between_the_President_and_Cuban_President_Ra%C3%BAl_Castro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Handshake_between_the_President_and_Cuban_President_Ra%C3%BAl_Castro.jpg)

The meeting between [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") and [Raul Castro](/wiki/Raul_Castro "Raul Castro") during the [Summit of the Americas](/wiki/7th_Summit_of_the_Americas "7th Summit of the Americas") in Panama City on April 11, 2015

The Obama presidency saw a major thaw in relations with Cuba, which the United States [embargoed](/wiki/United_States_embargo_against_Cuba "United States embargo against Cuba") following the [Cuban Revolution](/wiki/Cuban_Revolution "Cuban Revolution") and the 1962 [Cuban Missile Crisis](/wiki/Cuban_Missile_Crisis "Cuban Missile Crisis"). Starting in the spring of 2013 secret meetings were conducted between the United States and [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), with the meetings taking place in the neutral locations of Canada and [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City").[[381]](#cite_note-387) The Vatican was consulted initially in 2013 as [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") advised the US and Cuba to [exchange prisoners](/wiki/Prisoner_swap "Prisoner swap") as a gesture of goodwill.[[382]](#cite_note-The_Washington_Post-388) On December 10, 2013, Cuban President [Raúl Castro](/wiki/Raul_Castro "Raul Castro"), in a significant public moment, shook hands with and greeted Obama at [Nelson Mandela's memorial service](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Nelson_Mandela "Death and state funeral of Nelson Mandela") in [Johannesburg](/wiki/Johannesburg "Johannesburg").[[383]](#cite_note-389) In December 2014, Cuba released [Alan Gross](/wiki/Alan_Gross "Alan Gross") in exchange for the remaining members of the [Cuban Five](/wiki/Cuban_Five "Cuban Five").[[382]](#cite_note-The_Washington_Post-388) That same month, President Obama ordered the restoration of diplomatic ties with Cuba.[[384]](#cite_note-bakercuba-390) Obama stated that he was normalizing relationships because the economic embargo had been ineffective in persuading Cuba to develop a democratic society.[[385]](#cite_note-391) In May 2015, Cuba was taken off the United States's list of [State Sponsors of Terrorism](/wiki/State_Sponsors_of_Terrorism "State Sponsors of Terrorism").[[386]](#cite_note-us-officially-392) In August 2015, following the restoration of official diplomatic relations, the United States and Cuba reopened their respective embassies.[[387]](#cite_note-oppman-393) In March 2016, Obama visited Cuba, making him the first American president to set foot on the island since [Calvin Coolidge](/wiki/Calvin_Coolidge "Calvin Coolidge").[[388]](#cite_note-jpace-394) In 2017, Obama ended the "[wet feet, dry feet policy](/wiki/Wet_feet,_dry_feet_policy "Wet feet, dry feet policy")", which had given special rights to Cuban immigrants to the United States.[[389]](#cite_note-lastbigcubamove1-395) The restored ties between Cuba and the US were seen as a boon to broader [Latin America–United States relations](/wiki/Latin_America%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Latin America–United States relations"), as Latin American leaders unanimously approved of the move.[[390]](#cite_note-shorsley-396)[[391]](#cite_note-welsh-397) Presidential candidate Donald Trump promised to reverse the Obama policies and return to a hard line on Cuba.[[392]](#cite_note-398)

### Iran

Main article: [Iran–United States relations during the Obama administration](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93United_States_relations_during_the_Obama_administration "Iran–United States relations during the Obama administration")

President Obama announces an agreement on the Iran nuclear deal, 14 July 2015

Iran and the United States have had a poor [relationship](/wiki/Iran%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Iran–United States relations") since the 1979 [Iranian Revolution](/wiki/Iranian_Revolution "Iranian Revolution") and the [Iran hostage crisis](/wiki/Iran_hostage_crisis "Iran hostage crisis"), and tensions continued during the Obama administration due to issues such as the [Iranian nuclear program](/wiki/Nuclear_program_of_Iran "Nuclear program of Iran") and [Iran's alleged sponsorship of terrorism](/wiki/Iran_and_state-sponsored_terrorism "Iran and state-sponsored terrorism"). On taking office, Obama focused on [negotiations](/wiki/Negotiations_leading_to_the_Joint_Comprehensive_Plan_of_Action "Negotiations leading to the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action") with Iran over the status of its nuclear program, working with the other [P5+1](/wiki/P5%2B1 "P5+1") powers to adopt a multilateral agreement.[[393]](#cite_note-coheniran-399) Obama's stance differed dramatically from the more hawkish position of his predecessor, [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"),[[394]](#cite_note-fromero-400) as well as the stated positions of most of Obama's rivals in the 2008 presidential campaign.[[395]](#cite_note-401) In June 2013, [Hasan Rouhani](/wiki/Hasan_Rouhani "Hasan Rouhani") won [election](/wiki/2013_Iranian_presidential_election "2013 Iranian presidential election") as the new [President of Iran](/wiki/President_of_Iran "President of Iran"), and Rouhani called for a continuation of talks on Iran's nuclear program.[[396]](#cite_note-irannatimeline-402) In November 2013, Iran and the P5 announced an interim agreement,[[396]](#cite_note-irannatimeline-402) and in April 2015, negotiators announced that a framework agreement had been reached.[[397]](#cite_note-403) Congressional Republicans, who along with Israeli prime minister [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") had strongly opposed the negotiations,[[398]](#cite_note-404) attempted but failed to pass a Congressional resolution rejecting the six-nation accord.[[399]](#cite_note-steinhauer2-405) Under the [Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action](/wiki/Joint_Comprehensive_Plan_of_Action "Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action"), Iran promised to limit its nuclear program and to provide access to [International Atomic Energy Agency](/wiki/International_Atomic_Energy_Agency "International Atomic Energy Agency") inspectors, while the US and other countries agreed to reduce sanctions on Iran.[[400]](#cite_note-wbroad-406) The partisan fight over the Iran nuclear deal exemplified a broader ideological disagreement regarding American foreign policy in the Middle East and how to handle adversarial regimes, as many opponents of the deal considered Iran to be an implacably hostile adversary who would inevitably break any agreement.[[401]](#cite_note-mfisher-407)

### Arab Spring and its aftermath

See also: [Arab Spring](/wiki/Arab_Spring "Arab Spring")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Arab_Spring_and_Regional_Conflict_Map.svg/330px-Arab_Spring_and_Regional_Conflict_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Arab_Spring_and_Regional_Conflict_Map.svg)

Most [Arab states](/wiki/Arab_states "Arab states") experienced turmoil during the [Arab Spring](/wiki/Arab_Spring "Arab Spring").  
  Civil war   Government overthrown multiple times   Government overthrown   Protests and governmental changes   Major protests   Minor protests

After a sudden revolution in Tunisia in 2011,[[402]](#cite_note-408) protests occurred in almost every [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") state. The wave of demonstrations became known as the [Arab Spring](/wiki/Arab_Spring "Arab Spring"), and the handling of the Arab Spring played a major role in Obama's foreign policy.[[403]](#cite_note-ianblack-409) After three weeks of [unrest](/wiki/Egyptian_Revolution_of_2011 "Egyptian Revolution of 2011"), Egyptian president [Hosni Mubarak](/wiki/Hosni_Mubarak "Hosni Mubarak") resigned at the urging of President Obama.[[404]](#cite_note-hcooper-410) General [Abdel Fattah el-Sisi](/wiki/Abdel_Fattah_el-Sisi "Abdel Fattah el-Sisi") eventually took power from Egyptian president [Mohammed Morsi](/wiki/Mohammed_Morsi "Mohammed Morsi") in a [2013 coup d'état](/wiki/2013_Egyptian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "2013 Egyptian coup d'état"), prompting the US to cut off arms shipments to its long-time ally.[[405]](#cite_note-kliptak-411) However, Obama resumed the shipments in 2015.[[405]](#cite_note-kliptak-411) Yemen experienced a [revolution](/wiki/Yemeni_Revolution "Yemeni Revolution") and then [civil war](/wiki/Yemeni_Civil_War_(2015%E2%80%93present) "Yemeni Civil War (2015–present)"), leading to a [Saudi military campaign](/wiki/Saudi_Arabian-led_intervention_in_Yemen "Saudi Arabian-led intervention in Yemen") that received logistical and intelligence assistance from the United States.[[406]](#cite_note-hnaylor-412) The Obama administration announced its intention to review US military assistance to [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") after Saudi warplanes [targeted a funeral](/wiki/2016_Sana%27a_funeral_airstrike "2016 Sana'a funeral airstrike") in Yemen's capital Sanaa, killing more than 140 people.[[407]](#cite_note-413) The UN accused the Saudi-led coalition of "complete disregard for human life".[[408]](#cite_note-414)[[409]](#cite_note-415)[[410]](#cite_note-416)

#### Libya

See also: [Libyan civil war (2011)](/wiki/Libyan_civil_war_(2011) "Libyan civil war (2011)")

Libya was strongly affected by the Arab Spring. Anti-government protests broke out in [Benghazi](/wiki/Benghazi "Benghazi"), Libya, in February 2011,[[411]](#cite_note-417) and the [Gaddafi](/wiki/Muammar_Gaddafi "Muammar Gaddafi") government responded with military force.[[412]](#cite_note-418) The Obama administration initially resisted calls to take strong action[[413]](#cite_note-419) but relented after the Arab League requested Western intervention in Libya.[[414]](#cite_note-420) In March 2011, international reaction to Gaddafi's military crackdown culminated in a [United Nations resolution](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_1973 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 1973") to enforce a [no fly zone](/wiki/No_fly_zone "No fly zone") in Libya. Obama authorized US forces to participate in [international air attacks](/wiki/2011_military_intervention_in_Libya "2011 military intervention in Libya") on Libyan air defenses using [Tomahawk cruise missiles](/wiki/Tomahawk_(missile_family) "Tomahawk (missile family)") to establish the protective zone.[[415]](#cite_note-421)[[416]](#cite_note-422) The intervention was led by [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), but [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") and three [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") nations also participated in the mission.[[417]](#cite_note-423) With coalition support, the rebels took Tripoli the following August.[[418]](#cite_note-424) The Libyan campaign culminated in the toppling of the Gaddafi regime, but Libya experienced [turmoil](/wiki/Libyan_Civil_War_(2014%E2%80%93present) "Libyan Civil War (2014–present)") in the aftermath of the [civil war](/wiki/Libyan_civil_war_(2011) "Libyan civil war (2011)").[[419]](#cite_note-bbclibya-425) Obama's intervention in Libya [provoked criticism](/wiki/US_domestic_reactions_to_the_2011_military_intervention_in_Libya "US domestic reactions to the 2011 military intervention in Libya") from members of Congress and ignited a debate over the applicability of the [War Powers Resolution](/wiki/War_Powers_Resolution "War Powers Resolution").[[420]](#cite_note-csavage-426) In September 2012, Islamic militants [attacked](/wiki/2012_Benghazi_attack "2012 Benghazi attack") the American consulate in Benghazi, killing [Ambassador](/wiki/United_States_Ambassador_to_Libya "United States Ambassador to Libya") [J. Christopher Stevens](/wiki/J._Christopher_Stevens "J. Christopher Stevens") and three other Americans.[[421]](#cite_note-reuters1-427) Republicans strongly criticized the Obama administration's handling of the Benghazi attack, and established a [select committee](/wiki/United_States_House_Select_Committee_on_Benghazi "United States House Select Committee on Benghazi") in the House to investigate the attack.[[422]](#cite_note-wheaton-428) After his presidency, Obama acknowledged his "worst mistake" of his presidency was being unable to anticipate the aftermath of ousting Gaddafi.[[423]](#cite_note-429)

#### Syrian civil war

See also: [Syrian civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war") and [US intervention in the Syrian civil war](/wiki/US_intervention_in_the_Syrian_civil_war "US intervention in the Syrian civil war")

Syria was one of the states most heavily affected by the Arab Spring, and by the second half of March 2011, major anti-government protests were being held in Syria.[[424]](#cite_note-430) Though Syria had long been an adversary of the United States, Obama argued that unilateral military action to topple the [Bashar al-Assad](/wiki/Bashar_al-Assad "Bashar al-Assad") regime would be a mistake.[[425]](#cite_note-431) As the protests continued, Syria fell into a protracted [civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war"),[[426]](#cite_note-432) and the United States supported the [Syrian opposition](/wiki/Syrian_opposition "Syrian opposition") against the Assad regime.[[427]](#cite_note-jpace2-433) US criticism of Assad intensified after the [Ghouta chemical attack](/wiki/Ghouta_chemical_attack "Ghouta chemical attack"), eventually resulting in a Russian-backed deal that saw the Syrian government relinquish its chemical weapons.[[428]](#cite_note-zbeauchamp-434) In the chaos of the Syrian Civil War, an Islamist group known as [Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant](/wiki/Islamic_State_of_Iraq_and_the_Levant "Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant") (ISIL) took control of large portions of Syria and Iraq.[[429]](#cite_note-kshaeen-435) ISIL, which had originated as [al-Qaeda in Iraq](/wiki/Al-Qaeda_in_Iraq "Al-Qaeda in Iraq") under the leadership of [Abu Musab al-Zarqawi](/wiki/Abu_Musab_al-Zarqawi "Abu Musab al-Zarqawi"),[[296]](#cite_note-Isis1-302) eventually challenged [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") as the most prominent global [terrorist](/wiki/Terrorist "Terrorist") group during Obama's second term.[[430]](#cite_note-elabottisil-436) Starting in 2014, the Obama administration launched [air strikes](/wiki/American-led_intervention_in_Syria "American-led intervention in Syria") against ISIL and trained anti-ISIL soldiers, while continuing to oppose Assad's regime.[[427]](#cite_note-jpace2-433)[[428]](#cite_note-zbeauchamp-434) The Obama administration also cooperated with [Syrian Kurds](/wiki/Kurds_in_Syria "Kurds in Syria") in opposing the ISIL, straining relations with [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), which accused the Syrian Kurds of working with the [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurds "Kurds") terrorist groups [inside Turkey](/wiki/Kurds_in_Turkey "Kurds in Turkey").[[431]](#cite_note-cunninghamkurds1-437) Russia launched its own [military intervention](/wiki/Russian_military_intervention_in_the_Syrian_Civil_War "Russian military intervention in the Syrian Civil War") to aid Assad's regime, creating a complicated multi-party [proxy war](/wiki/Proxy_war "Proxy war"), though the United States and Russia sometimes cooperated to fight ISIL.[[432]](#cite_note-syriaproxywar-438) In November 2015, Obama announced a plan to resettle at least 10,000 [Syrian refugees](/wiki/Refugees_of_the_Syrian_Civil_War "Refugees of the Syrian Civil War") in the United States.[[192]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-198) Obama's "light-footprint" approach to the Syrian conflict was criticized by many as the Syrian Civil War became a major humanitarian catastrophe, but supporters of Obama argued that he deserved credit for keeping the United States out of another costly ground war in the Middle East.[[433]](#cite_note-mspetalnick1-439)[[434]](#cite_note-usher1-440)[[298]](#cite_note-parsonshennigan1-304)

### Foreign and domestic surveillance

See also: [Barack Obama on mass surveillance](/wiki/Barack_Obama_on_mass_surveillance "Barack Obama on mass surveillance")

The Obama administration inherited several government surveillance programs from the Bush administration, and Obama attempted to strike a balance between protecting civil liberties and tracking terrorist threats, but Obama's continuation of many programs disappointed many civil libertarians.[[435]](#cite_note-mshear2-441) *The New York Times* reported in 2009 that the NSA had been intercepting communications of American citizens including a congressman, although the [Justice Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") believed that the NSA had corrected its errors.[[436]](#cite_note-442) In 2011, Obama signed a four-year extension of some provisions of the [Patriot Act](/wiki/Patriot_Act "Patriot Act").[[437]](#cite_note-tcohen-443) In June 2013 the existence of [PRISM](/wiki/PRISM_(surveillance_program) "PRISM (surveillance program)"), a [clandestine](/wiki/Clandestine_operation "Clandestine operation") [mass](/wiki/Mass_surveillance "Mass surveillance") [electronic surveillance](/wiki/Computer_surveillance "Computer surveillance") [data mining](/wiki/Data_mining "Data mining") program operated by the United States [National Security Agency](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency") (NSA) since 2007, was [leaked](/wiki/News_leak "News leak") by NSA contractor [Edward Snowden](/wiki/Edward_Snowden "Edward Snowden"), who warned that the extent of mass data collection was far greater than the public knew.[[438]](#cite_note-ibtimes_17_june-444) In the face of international outrage, US government officials defended the PRISM surveillance program by asserting it could not be used on domestic targets without a [warrant](/wiki/Search_warrant "Search warrant"), that it helped to prevent acts of terrorism, and that it received independent oversight from the federal government's [executive](/wiki/U.S._Executive_Branch#Executive_branch "U.S. Executive Branch"), [judicial](/wiki/U.S._Executive_Branch#Judicial_branch "U.S. Executive Branch") and [legislative](/wiki/U.S._Executive_Branch#Legislative_branch "U.S. Executive Branch") branches.[[439]](#cite_note-445) In June 2013, Obama stated that the NSA's data gathering practices constitute "a circumscribed, narrow system directed at us being able to protect our people."[[440]](#cite_note-cbsberlin-446) In 2015, Obama signed the [USA Freedom Act](/wiki/USA_Freedom_Act "USA Freedom Act"), which extended several provisions of the Patriot Act but ended the collection of bulk telephone records by the NSA.[[435]](#cite_note-mshear2-441)[[441]](#cite_note-fthorp-447)

## Ethics

### Lobbying reform

See also: [Lobbying in the United States](/wiki/Lobbying_in_the_United_States "Lobbying in the United States")

Early in [his presidential campaign](/wiki/Barack_Obama_2008_presidential_campaign "Barack Obama 2008 presidential campaign"), Obama stated that lobbyists "won't find a job in my White House", but softened his stance after taking office.[[442]](#cite_note-lobbyists1-448) On January 21, 2009, Obama issued an executive order for all future appointees to his administration, which ordered that no appointee who was a registered lobbyist within the two years before his appointment could participate on matters in which he lobbied for a period of two years after the date of appointment.[[15]](#cite_note-EthicsOrder-17) Three formal waivers were initially issued in early 2009, out of 800 executive appointments:[[443]](#cite_note-ABCWaivers-449) The [Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington](/wiki/Citizens_for_Responsibility_and_Ethics_in_Washington "Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington") criticized the administration, claiming that Obama retreated from his own ethics rules barring lobbyists from working on the issues about which they lobbied during the previous two years by issuing waivers.[[444]](#cite_note-USATodayLobbyists-450) A 2015 Politico investigation found that, while Obama had instituted incremental reforms and the number of lobbyists fell during Obama's presidency, Obama had failed to close the "revolving door" of officials moving between government and business.[[445]](#cite_note-gersteinlobbying-451) However, the Obama administration avoided "conflict of interest" scandals that previous administrations had experienced, in part due to the administration's lobbyist rules.[[446]](#cite_note-eilperinlobbyists-452)

### Transparency

Obama presents his first [weekly address](/wiki/Weekly_address "Weekly address") as President of the United States, discussing the [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009").

Obama promised that he would run the "most transparent" administration in US history, with mixed results.[[447]](#cite_note-jrarnold-453) On taking office, the Obama administration said that all executive orders, non-emergency legislation, and proclamations would be posted to the official [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") website, [whitehouse.gov](/wiki/Whitehouse.gov "Whitehouse.gov"), allowing the public to review and comment for five days before the president signs the legislation,[[448]](#cite_note-454) but this pledge was twice broken during Obama's first month in office.[[449]](#cite_note-455)[[450]](#cite_note-456) On January 21, 2009, by executive order, Obama revoked [Executive Order 13233](/wiki/Executive_Order_13233 "Executive Order 13233"), which had limited access to the records of former United States presidents.[[451]](#cite_note-457) Obama issued instructions to all agencies and departments in his administration to "adopt a presumption in favor" of [Freedom of Information Act](/wiki/Freedom_of_Information_Act_(United_States) "Freedom of Information Act (United States)") requests.[[452]](#cite_note-458) These actions helped the rate of classification fall to record lows during the Obama administration.[[447]](#cite_note-jrarnold-453) In April 2009, the [United States Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") released four legal memos from the Bush administration describing in detail [controversial interrogation methods](/wiki/Enhanced_interrogation_techniques "Enhanced interrogation techniques") the CIA had used on prisoners suspected of terrorism.[[453]](#cite_note-459)[[454]](#cite_note-460) The Obama administration also introduced the Open Government Directive, which encouraged government agencies to publish data and collaborate with the public, and the Open Government Partnership, which advocated open government norms.[[447]](#cite_note-jrarnold-453) However, Obama continued to make use of secret memos and the [state secrets privilege](/wiki/State_secrets_privilege "State secrets privilege"), and he continued to prosecute whistleblowers.[[447]](#cite_note-jrarnold-453)

The Obama administration was much more aggressive than the Bush and other previous administrations in their response to [whistleblowing](/wiki/Whistleblowing "Whistleblowing") and leaks to the press,[[455]](#cite_note-shane2-461) prompting critics to describe the Obama administration's crackdown as a "war on whistleblowers".[[456]](#cite_note-WP-462)[[457]](#cite_note-nation-463) Several people were charged under the previously rarely used leak-related provisions of the [Espionage Act of 1917](/wiki/Espionage_Act_of_1917 "Espionage Act of 1917"), including [Thomas Andrews Drake](/wiki/Thomas_Andrews_Drake "Thomas Andrews Drake"), a former [National Security Agency](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency") employee,[[458]](#cite_note-naka2-464)[[459]](#cite_note-harri1-465) [Stephen Jin-Woo Kim](/wiki/Stephen_Jin-Woo_Kim "Stephen Jin-Woo Kim"), a [State Department](/wiki/State_Department "State Department") contractor,[[460]](#cite_note-shane3-466) and [Jeffrey Sterling](/wiki/Jeffrey_Alexander_Sterling "Jeffrey Alexander Sterling"). Others prosecuted for leaking information include [Shamai Leibowitz](/wiki/Shamai_Leibowitz "Shamai Leibowitz"), a contract linguist for the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation"),[[461]](#cite_note-WaPo1-467) [John Kiriakou](/wiki/John_Kiriakou "John Kiriakou"), a former CIA analyst,[[462]](#cite_note-Shane-Kiriakou-468) and [Chelsea Manning](/wiki/Chelsea_Manning "Chelsea Manning"), an intelligence analyst for the [US Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") whose [trial](/wiki/United_States_v._Manning "United States v. Manning") received wide coverage.[[463]](#cite_note-469) Most notably, [Edward Snowden](/wiki/Edward_Snowden "Edward Snowden"), a technical contractor for the [NSA](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency"), was charged with theft and the unauthorized [disclosure of classified information](/wiki/Global_surveillance_disclosures_(2013%E2%80%93present) "Global surveillance disclosures (2013–present)") to columnist [Glenn Greenwald](/wiki/Glenn_Greenwald "Glenn Greenwald").[[464]](#cite_note-470) Snowden's disclosures provoked [wide array of reactions](/wiki/Reactions_to_global_surveillance_disclosures "Reactions to global surveillance disclosures"); many called for Snowden to be pardoned, while others called him a traitor.[[465]](#cite_note-afredrick-471)[[466]](#cite_note-liptaksnowden-472)

### 2010 mid-term elections

Main article: [2010 United States elections](/wiki/2010_United_States_elections "2010 United States elections")

Attacking Obama relentlessly, emphasizing the stalled economy, and enjoying the anger of the [Tea Party Movement](/wiki/Tea_Party_Movement "Tea Party Movement"), Republicans had a [red wave](/wiki/Wave_elections_in_the_United_States "Wave elections in the United States") in the 2010 mid-term elections, [winning control of the House](/wiki/2010_United_States_House_of_Representatives_elections "2010 United States House of Representatives elections") and gaining seats in the Senate. After the election, John Boehner replaced Nancy Pelosi as Speaker of the House, and Pelosi became the new House Minority Leader. Boehner pledged to repeal Obamacare and cut federal spending.[[467]](#cite_note-pkane-476)

Obama called the elections "humbling" and a "shellacking", arguing that the defeat came because not enough Americans had felt the effects of the economic recovery.[[468]](#cite_note-477) The newly empowered House Republicans quickly confronted Obama on issues such as Obamacare and the debt ceiling.[[148]](#cite_note-wallsten-154) The Republican victory in the election also gave Republicans the upper hand in the [redistricting](/wiki/Redistricting "Redistricting") that occurred after the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[469]](#cite_note-giroux-478)

### 2012 re-election campaign

Main articles: [Barack Obama 2012 presidential campaign](/wiki/Barack_Obama_2012_presidential_campaign "Barack Obama 2012 presidential campaign") and [2012 United States presidential election](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election")

Further information: [2012 United States elections](/wiki/2012_United_States_elections "2012 United States elections"), [2012 Democratic Party presidential primaries](/wiki/2012_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "2012 Democratic Party presidential primaries"), and [2012 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/2012_Democratic_National_Convention "2012 Democratic National Convention")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/ElectoralCollege2012.svg/500px-ElectoralCollege2012.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ElectoralCollege2012.svg)

President Obama defeated Republican [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney") in the 2012 presidential election.

On April 4, 2011, Obama announced that he would seek [re-election in 2012](/wiki/Barack_Obama_2012_presidential_campaign "Barack Obama 2012 presidential campaign"). He did not face any significant rivals for the Democratic nomination. His Republican opponent was [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney"), a former governor of Massachusetts. Romney called for lower taxes, spending cuts, an increase in defense spending, and a repeal of [Obamacare](/wiki/Obamacare "Obamacare") (even though it was based on a [Massachusetts healthcare plan](/wiki/Massachusetts_health_care_reform "Massachusetts health care reform") developed under Romney).[[470]](#cite_note-romneyplatform-479) Obama's campaign was based in Chicago and run by many former members of the White House staff and members of the successful [2008 campaign](/wiki/Barack_Obama_2008_presidential_campaign "Barack Obama 2008 presidential campaign").[[471]](#cite_note-480) Obama won re-election with 332 electoral votes and 51.1% of the popular vote, while Romney received 206 electoral votes and 47.2% of the popular vote, making him the first person since [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower") to twice win 51 percent of the vote.[[472]](#cite_note-giroux2-481) According to exit polls, Obama won a majority of the vote from women, blacks, Hispanics, Asians, people under 45, people making less than $50,000 per year, people inhabiting large or mid-sized cities, liberals, moderates, the unmarried, gays, and people with no college education, some college education, or graduate degrees.[[473]](#cite_note-2012exitpolls-482) In the concurrent [congressional elections](/wiki/2012_United_States_elections "2012 United States elections"), the Democrats also picked up seats in both houses of Congress, but Republicans retained control of the House.

### 2014 mid-term elections

Main article: [2014 United States elections](/wiki/2014_United_States_elections "2014 United States elections")

In the 2014 mid-term elections, Republicans had another [red wave](/wiki/Wave_elections_in_the_United_States "Wave elections in the United States"), [winning control of the Senate](/wiki/2014_United_States_Senate_elections "2014 United States Senate elections"), gaining seats in the House, and picked up several governorships.[[474]](#cite_note-montanaro-483) Mitch McConnell replaced Harry Reid as Senate Majority Leader, while Reid became the Senate Minority Leader. Republican control of the Senate gave the party the power to block Obama's executive and judicial nominees.[[41]](#cite_note-everett1-43) The Republican waves in 2010 and 2014 defeated many young Democratic candidates, weakening the farm team of several state Democratic parties.[[475]](#cite_note-stolberg-484)

### 2016 elections and transition period

Main article: [2016 United States elections](/wiki/2016_United_States_elections "2016 United States elections")

Further information: [Hillary Clinton 2016 presidential campaign](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton_2016_presidential_campaign "Hillary Clinton 2016 presidential campaign"), [Donald Trump 2016 presidential campaign](/wiki/Donald_Trump_2016_presidential_campaign "Donald Trump 2016 presidential campaign"), [2016 United States presidential election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election"), and [First presidential transition of Donald Trump](/wiki/First_presidential_transition_of_Donald_Trump "First presidential transition of Donald Trump")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/ElectoralCollege2016.svg/500px-ElectoralCollege2016.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ElectoralCollege2016.svg)

Republican [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") defeated Democrat [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") in the 2016 presidential election.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Barack_Obama_meets_with_Donald_Trump_in_the_Oval_Office_%28crop_2%29.jpg/250px-Barack_Obama_meets_with_Donald_Trump_in_the_Oval_Office_%28crop_2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barack_Obama_meets_with_Donald_Trump_in_the_Oval_Office_(crop_2).jpg)

Outgoing president Barack Obama and President-elect [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") in the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office") on November 10, 2016

The 2016 elections took place on November 8. Obama was term-limited in 2016 due to the [22nd Amendment](/wiki/Twenty-second_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twenty-second Amendment to the United States Constitution"), though Obama's approval ratings may have impacted his party's ability to win the race.[[476]](#cite_note-kondik-485) In June 2016, with the [Democratic primaries](/wiki/2016_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "2016 Democratic Party presidential primaries") nearly complete, Obama endorsed former secretary of state [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") as his successor.[[477]](#cite_note-whyobamawaited-486) However, according to Glenn Thrush of [Politico](/wiki/Politico "Politico"), Obama had long supported Clinton as his preferred successor, and Obama dissuaded Vice President Biden from running against Clinton.[[478]](#cite_note-partyoftwo-487) Obama spoke in favor of Clinton at the [2016 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/2016_Democratic_National_Convention "2016 Democratic National Convention"), and he continued to campaign for Clinton and other Democrats in the months leading up to Election Day.[[479]](#cite_note-mrhodan1-488) However, in the general election, Clinton was defeated by Republican nominee [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), who prominently [questioned](/wiki/Barack_Obama_citizenship_conspiracy_theories "Barack Obama citizenship conspiracy theories") Obama's place of birth during Obama's first term.[[480]](#cite_note-asilverleib-489) In the concurrent [congressional elections](/wiki/2016_United_States_elections "2016 United States elections"), Republicans also retained control of their majorities in the [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") and the [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). During the eight years of Obama's presidency, the Democratic Party experienced a net loss of 1,041 governorships and state and federal legislative seats.[[481]](#cite_note-490) Ronald Brownstein of *[The Atlantic](/wiki/The_Atlantic "The Atlantic")* noted that these losses were similar to those of other post-World War II two-term presidents.[[482]](#cite_note-brownsteain11217-491)[[483]](#cite_note-cmalone1-492)

Trump and Obama frequently communicated during the [transition period](/wiki/First_presidential_transition_of_Donald_Trump "First presidential transition of Donald Trump"), and Trump stated that he sought Obama's advice regarding presidential appointments.[[484]](#cite_note-tnaftali1-493) However, President-elect Trump also criticized some of Obama's actions, including Obama's refusal to veto a UN Resolution condemning Israel settlements.[[485]](#cite_note-Wagnerroadblocks1-494) In his [farewell address](/wiki/Barack_Obama%27s_farewell_address "Barack Obama's farewell address"), Obama expressed concerns about a divisive political environment, economic inequality, and racism, but remained optimistic about the future.[[486]](#cite_note-wpfarewell-495)[[487]](#cite_note-nytfarewell-496)

After his transition period, Obama entered office with an approval rating of 82% according to [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup_(company) "Gallup (company)"),[[489]](#cite_note-latimesar-498) Obama's approval rating fell to 69% after he took office and announced his first policy decisions.[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499) Obama received the support of 90% of Democrats, 60% of independents, and 40% of Republicans in January 2009 polls.[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499) By December 2009, Obama's approval rating had fallen to 51%, with Obama receiving approval from roughly 85% of Democrats, 45% of independents, and just 18% of Republicans.[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499) In July 2010, after the passage of the Dodd-Frank and Obamacare, Obama's approval rating stood at 45%, with 47% disapproving.[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499) Obama's approval rating would remain stable until the 2010 elections,[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499) when Republicans won major gains in both houses of Congress and took control of the House.[[467]](#cite_note-pkane-476) Obama's approval ratings climbed back to 50% in January 2011, but fell to 40% in August 2011 following the [2011 debt-ceiling crisis](/wiki/United_States_debt-ceiling_crisis_of_2011 "United States debt-ceiling crisis of 2011").[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499) Obama's approval ratings slowly increased during 2012, and they rose above 50% shortly before the 2012 election, in which Obama defeated [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney").[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499) After his re-election, Obama's approval ratings reached 57%, but that number fell into the low 40s after the [federal government shutdown](/wiki/United_States_federal_government_shutdown_of_2013 "United States federal government shutdown of 2013") in October 2013.[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499) Obama's approval ratings remained in the mid-to-low 40s until the [2014 elections](/wiki/2014_United_States_elections "2014 United States elections"), when Republicans won gains in both houses of Congress and took control of the Senate.[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499) In 2015, Obama's approval ratings climbed to the mid-to-high 40s, with his approval and disapproval ratings roughly matching each other.[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499) His approval ratings rose into the 50s during the 2016 presidential campaign, and Obama registered a 57% approval rating in November 2016.[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499) In a Gallup poll taken in the final week of his presidency, Obama registered a 95% approval rating with Democrats, a 61% approval rating with independents, and a 14% approval rating with Republicans.[[490]](#cite_note-Galluppollsb-499)

Obama's election also provoked a reaction to his race, birthplace, and religion. As president, Obama faced numerous taunts and racial innuendos, though most overt racist comments were limited to a small fringe.[[491]](#cite_note-snetter-500) [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") theorized that Obama had [been born](/wiki/Barack_Obama_citizenship_conspiracy_theories "Barack Obama citizenship conspiracy theories") in [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"); an April 2011 CNN poll taken shortly before Obama released his long-form birth certificate found that 40% of Republicans believed that Obama had been born in Kenya.[[480]](#cite_note-asilverleib-489) Many of these "birthers" argued that because Obama was (allegedly) not a citizen, he was not eligible to serve as president under the [natural-born-citizen](/wiki/Natural-born-citizen_clause "Natural-born-citizen clause") requirements of the Constitution. Despite Obama's release of his long-form birth certificate, which affirmed that Obama was born in Hawaii, a 2015 [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN") poll found that 20% of Americans believed that Obama was born outside of the country.[[492]](#cite_note-jagiesta-501) Many also [claimed](/wiki/Barack_Obama_religion_conspiracy_theories "Barack Obama religion conspiracy theories") that Obama practiced [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), and a 2015 CNN poll found that 29% of Americans and 43% of Republicans believed Obama to be a Muslim.[[492]](#cite_note-jagiesta-501) Even prior to his election as president, Obama had clarified that he was a long-time member of a church affiliated with the [United Church of Christ](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ "United Church of Christ"), a [mainline Protestant](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant") denomination.[[493]](#cite_note-obamareligion-502)

In a January 2010 survey by the [Siena Research Institute](/wiki/Siena_Research_Institute "Siena Research Institute") at [Siena College](/wiki/Siena_College "Siena College") in [Loudonville, New York](/wiki/Loudonville,_New_York "Loudonville, New York")—one year into the Obama presidency—238 US history and political science professors ranked Obama 15th of 43 US presidents.[[494]](#cite_note-503) In a September 2010 survey by the United States Presidency Centre of the [Institute for the Study of the Americas](/wiki/Institute_for_the_Study_of_the_Americas "Institute for the Study of the Americas") at the [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London "University of London") [School of Advanced Study](/wiki/School_of_Advanced_Study "School of Advanced Study")—one year and eight months into the Obama presidency—47 unnamed respondents who were UK academic specialists on American history and politics ranked 40 of 42 US presidents from 1789 to 2009, not including Obama; if Obama had been included he would have ranked 8th, behind Harry S. Truman but ahead of Ronald Reagan and all other post-World War II US presidents.[[495]](#cite_note-504)[[496]](#cite_note-505)[[497]](#cite_note-506) In a June 2012 survey by *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")* magazine—three years and five months into the Obama presidency—ten selected American historians and biographers ranked Obama 10th of 20 US presidents since 1900.[[498]](#cite_note-507)[[499]](#cite_note-508) In an April 2013 survey by [History News Network](/wiki/History_News_Network "History News Network") (HNN) website in Seattle—four years and three months into the Obama presidency—203 scholars from 69 top US colleges and universities gave Obama a B− grade on an A–F scale.[[500]](#cite_note-509) A February 2015 [Brookings Institution](/wiki/Brookings_Institution "Brookings Institution") survey of members of the [American Political Science Association](/wiki/American_Political_Science_Association "American Political Science Association") put Obama in 18th place out of the 43 presidents.[[501]](#cite_note-rottinghaus-510) Additionally, a 2011 Gallup poll found that 5% of Americans saw Obama as the country's greatest president.[[502]](#cite_note-511)

As Obama left office, historians expressed various opinions about his effectiveness as president, with many noting that subsequent events would determine his ultimate legacy.[[503]](#cite_note-timehistorians1-512)[[504]](#cite_note-lewisdjupe1-513) There was universal agreement that Obama would long be remembered as the first African-American president.[[503]](#cite_note-timehistorians1-512)[[504]](#cite_note-lewisdjupe1-513)[[505]](#cite_note-feldmann1-514) Many noted that Obama presided over an economic recovery and passed major domestic legislation, but failed to bridge a partisan divide and left office with his party in a weakened state.[[503]](#cite_note-timehistorians1-512)

2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Congress3_6-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Congress3_6-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-Congress3_6-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-Congress3_6-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-Congress3_6-4) 17 days of the 111th Congress (January 3, 2009 – January 19, 2009) took place under President Bush, and 17 days of the 115th Congress (January 3, 2017 – January 19, 2017) took place during Obama's second term.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-fiscalyear_102-0)** The income, outlay, and deficit numbers reflect [fiscal years](/wiki/Fiscal_year "Fiscal year") which last from October to September; for example, the 2014 fiscal year lasted from October 2013 to September 2014.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-Clintontaxes_137-0)** Numbers reflect post-[OBRA 93](/wiki/Omnibus_Budget_Reconciliation_Act_of_1993 "Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act of 1993") tax brackets.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-Bushtaxes_138-0)** Numbers reflect post-[Bush tax cuts](/wiki/Bush_tax_cuts "Bush tax cuts") tax brackets.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-Obamataxes_139-0)** Numbers reflect post-[American Taxpayer Relief Act of 2012](/wiki/American_Taxpayer_Relief_Act_of_2012 "American Taxpayer Relief Act of 2012") tax brackets.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-Ryan2015_473-0)** John Boehner resigned as Speaker of the House on October 29, 2015. [Paul Ryan](/wiki/Paul_Ryan "Paul Ryan") was [elected](/wiki/October_2015_Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives_election "October 2015 Speaker of the United States House of Representatives election") to replace Boehner as Speaker of the House on October 29, 2015.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-Congress_474-0)** Democratic seats at the start of each session of Congress. Independents caucusing with the Democratic Party (Senators [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders"), [Joe Lieberman](/wiki/Joe_Lieberman "Joe Lieberman"), and [Angus King](/wiki/Angus_King "Angus King")) are counted as Democrats for the purposes of this table. Throughout Obama's presidency, there were a total of 100 Senate seats in 435 House seats, so a Democratic majority in the Senate required 50 seats (since Democratic vice president [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") could provide the [tie-breaking vote](/wiki/List_of_tie-breaking_votes_cast_by_the_vice_president_of_the_United_States "List of tie-breaking votes cast by the vice president of the United States")), and a Democratic majority in the House required 218 seats (assuming no vacancies).
9. **[^](#cite_ref-Congress2_475-0)** In 2009, Democrats briefly gained a "[filibuster](/wiki/Filibuster_in_the_United_States_Senate "Filibuster in the United States Senate")-proof" 60 Senate seats after [Al Franken](/wiki/Al_Franken "Al Franken") won an [extremely close election](/wiki/United_States_Senate_election_in_Minnesota,_2008 "United States Senate election in Minnesota, 2008") and [Arlen Specter](/wiki/Arlen_Specter "Arlen Specter") switched parties, but the number of Senate Democrats went down to 59 seats after [Scott Brown](/wiki/Scott_Brown_(politician) "Scott Brown (politician)") won a [January 2010 special election](/wiki/United_States_Senate_special_election_in_Massachusetts,_2010 "United States Senate special election in Massachusetts, 2010") in Massachusetts.
